---
title: "Thesis analysis"
author: "Thomas Hakman"
date: "20/06/2021"
output: html_document
---

``````{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, 
  message = FALSE, 
  warning = FALSE, 
  cache = FALSE
)
```

# Analysis of the PvZ data in R

**Part 1: packages**

Load packages with pacman package installer 
```{r}
library(pacman)
p_load(
  readxl,
  knitr,
  here,
  lubridate,
  scales,
  janitor,
  tidyverse,
  oii
)
```

**Part 2: data cleaning**


## Raw data

The raw data files from the original study (survey and telemetry) are taken from a zip compressed file on OSF: <https://osf.io/cjd6z/>. (A user ID column was manually removed from the survey data before uploading.) Start by downloading that file and unpacking it to the target directory:

EA sent out the survey in two waves: one in August of 2020, the second in September 2020. For each wave, there are 9 telemetry files, each one recording a different aspect of play events in Plants vs. Zombies: Battle for Neighborville and one file for the survey results.

These telemetry files contain info on PVZ play events from a long period of time, including for players who did not participate in the survey. We limit our analyses to the individuals who participated in the survey, and to the 2 weeks preceding the survey. However, the raw data files might be of interest for additional analyses.

## Process raw files

Because there were two of each telemetry file (waves), we create a function to read both waves' data into a single table:

```{r}
read_both_waves <- function(stub = "OII_PVZ_Authentications", ...) {
  # Filenames of wave 1 and 2 authentications files
  list.files(
    here("data-raw/ea"), pattern = stub, full.names = TRUE
  ) %>% 
    # Put wave 1 first
    rev() %>% 
    # Read into a list of data frames
    map(read_csv, ...) %>% 
    # Bind into one data frame with wave ID
    bind_rows(.id = "wave") %>%
    # Take only participants who filled out survey
    filter(player_id %in% survey$player_id)
}
```

### Survey

Because we only keep telemetry data of players who answered the survey, we'll load the survey first.
```{r}
survey <- read_excel(
  here("data-raw/ea/Oxford PvZ - Pilot Wave + Wave 1 Download.xlsx")
)

survey <- survey %>% 
  mutate(
    # Ensure date data type
    date = mdy_hm(date),
    # Need another name to use in filtering telemetry
    start_date = date
  ) %>% 
  # Other files use player_id
  rename(player_id = UID)
```

#### Clean and transform

Next, we give some sensible variable names and assign proper variable types for the survey.
```{r give-proper-variable-names}
# Rename variables
survey <- 
  survey %>% 
  select(
    
    # meta-info
    player_id,
    start_date, date,
    survey_duration = qtime,
    of_age = Q2,
    consent = Q3,
    
    # demographics
    played = Q1,
    country = hCountry,
    survey_language = PRELANG,
    age = D1,
    gender = D2,
    gender_other = D2r3oe,
    experience = D3,
    
    # well-being
    spane_ = starts_with("A1"), # general well-being in the two weeks
    straightliner_spane = hA1Straight,
    
    # motivations
    played_with_others = NSat1, # filter question that determines whether relatedness items were shown
    autonomy_ = NSat2r1:NSat2r3,
    competence_ = NSat2r4:NSat2r6,
    relatedness_ = NSat2r7:NSat2r9,
    enjoyment_ = NSat2r10:NSat2r13,
    extrinsic_ = NSat2r14:NSat2r17,
    straightliner_motivations = hNSat2Straight,
    
    # self-reported play
    active_play_hours = GT1c1,
    active_play_minutes = GT1c2,
    within_estimate = GT2,
    between_estimate = GT3,
    spane_game_ = starts_with("GT4")
  )

# Assign proper variable types and factor levels
survey <- survey %>%   
  mutate(
    across(
      c(
        player_id,
        of_age:survey_language,
        gender,
        played_with_others
      ),
      as.factor
    ),
    
    across(
      c(of_age:played, played_with_others),
      ~ fct_recode(
        .x,
        "Yes" = "1",
        "No" = "2"
      )
    ),
    
    gender = fct_recode(
      gender,
      "Male" = "1",
      "Female" = "2",
      "Other" = "3",
      "Prefer not to say" = "4"
    ),
    
    country = fct_recode(
      country,
      "US" = "1",
      "UK" = "2",
      "Canada" = "5"
    ),
    
    survey_language = fct_recode(
      survey_language,
      "English" = "1",
      "French" = "2"
    )
  )

# Reverse scored items
survey <- survey %>% 
  mutate(
    across(
      c(
        starts_with("spane"), # 1 was never
        between_estimate, #  those estimates also had 1: much more than typically
        within_estimate,
        relatedness_3,
        enjoyment_2,
        enjoyment_3
      ),
      ~ 8 - .x
    )
  )

# Indicators for straightliners
survey <- survey %>% 
  mutate(across(contains("straightliner"), ~.x==1))
```

Then we create mean indices for the scales.
SPANE has positive affect, negative affect, and an affect balance score (subtract negative from positive).
```{r create-scales}
# General SPANE
survey <- 
  survey %>% 
  mutate(
    spane_positive = rowMeans(
      select(
        .,
        spane_1,
        spane_3,
        spane_5,
        spane_7,
        spane_10,
        spane_12
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    spane_negative = rowMeans(
      select(
        .,
        spane_2,
        spane_4,
        spane_6,
        spane_8,
        spane_9,
        spane_11
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    spane_balance = spane_positive - spane_negative
  )

# Motivations
survey <- survey %>% 
  mutate(
    autonomy = rowMeans(
      select(., starts_with("autonomy")), na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    competence = rowMeans(
      select(., starts_with("competence")), na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    relatedness = rowMeans(
      select(., starts_with("relatedness")), na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    enjoyment = rowMeans(
      select(., starts_with("enjoyment")), na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    extrinsic = rowMeans(
      select(., starts_with("extrinsic")), na.rm = TRUE
    )
  )

# SPANE because of playing game
survey <- survey %>% 
  mutate(
    spane_game_positive = rowMeans(
      select(
        .,
        spane_game_1,
        spane_game_3,
        spane_game_5,
        spane_game_7,
        spane_game_10,
        spane_game_12
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    spane_game_negative = rowMeans(
      select(
        .,
        spane_game_2,
        spane_game_4,
        spane_game_6,
        spane_game_8,
        spane_game_9,
        spane_game_11
      ),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    spane_game_balance = spane_game_positive - spane_game_negative
  )

# Hours of estimated play
survey <- survey %>%
  mutate(active_play_minutes = active_play_minutes / 60) %>% 
  mutate(active_play = rowSums(select(., starts_with("active_play")), na.rm = T))
```


## GAME TIME

* `player_id` = Unique identifier for a player.
* `platform` = One of the major platforms, eg. PC, Playstation, Xbox.
* `game_start_time` = Session start date time.
* `game_end_time` = Session end date time.
* `game_mode` = The game mode, primarily multiplayer or single player.
* `game_type` = Notes if the game is offline/online and if it is splitscreen
* `game_level` = The level's name where the session took place.
* `game_session` = Unique identifier for a game session.

```{r cache = TRUE}
game_time_raw <- read_both_waves("OII_PVZ_Game_Time")
glimpse(game_time_raw)
```

However, some start times are missing, which won't allow us to compute duration for that session.
Whenever a `game_start_time` is missing, we'll set it to the end time of that session.
That creates game sessions with a duration of 0, which we'll set to `NA` again later.
(Note: `game_end_time` doesn't have missing values, which is why we can use `coalesce`)
```{r}
#is this only done to be able to calculate duration?

game_time_test <- game_time_raw %>% 
  mutate(
    game_start_time = coalesce(
      game_start_time, game_end_time
    )
  )

# now collapse the rows
game_time_test <- game_time_test %>% 
  group_by(player_id, game_session) %>% 
  mutate(
    game_start_time = min(game_start_time),
    game_end_time = max(game_end_time),
  ) %>% 
  # Take the first of each (identical) person-session combo
  slice(1) %>% 
  ungroup()

glimpse(game_time_test)
```

## game time filtered for 2 weeks

The raw game times were used in processing the other files, so we left processing it until here.

First, we check which game sessions happened within the 14 days a participant took a survey (the file is already filtered to only include participants who actually filled out the survey). 

Afterwards, we aggregate the remaining game sessions per participant to get to how many hours they played in the two weeks before the survey (i.e., only aggregate/summarize `game_session`s that happened within the past 14 days).

```{r}
game_time_2week <- game_time_test %>% 
  left_join(select(survey, player_id, start_date)) %>% 
  filter(game_start_time >= (start_date - days(14)))
```

```{r}
oii.freq(game_time_2week$game_level_key) 
```


# Here we merge game time with the characters data set in order to be able to calculate game time per game mode propperly 
(the social hub games are sometimes pvp games, and the only way to distuingish between them is not by level key but to see if there were points scored)

This means we first need to create the characters data set and link it to the game time 2 week data set.
After that we can conitnue with calculating game time per game mode and total game time per player(per mode)



### Authentications

The authentifications data set logs events when a player (`player_id`) logs into the game.
The variables are:

* `player_id` = Unique identifier for a player.
* `platform` = One of the major platforms, eg. PC, Playstation, Xbox.
* `date` = Date of the login.
* `country` = Country the login came from.

```{r cache = TRUE}
authentications_raw <- read_both_waves("OII_PVZ_Authentications")
glimpse(authentications_raw)
```

First, we only keep authentications of players who also participated in the survey.
Then we count how many times players logged in to PvZ.
In the survey, players reflected on their well-being and gaming in the past two weeks, so we'll only count those game events that occurred within 14 days prior to the time the participant took the survey.
The `date` variable in `authentications` doesn't have time, so we'll make it easy and also reduce `start_time` in the survey to a date (without time of day).
```{r}
authentications_2week <- authentications_raw %>% 
  # add the start date by player ID
  left_join(select(survey, player_id, start_date)) %>% 
  # only keep authentication events that occured within 14 days before the survey was taken
  filter(date >= (as_date(start_date) - days(14)) ) #%>% 
  #count(player_id, name = "authentications")
```

```{r}
authentications_players <- authentications_2week %>% 
count(player_id, name = "authentications")
```

### Characters

We do the same for the performance stats in the `characters` data set.
The data set doesn't have a start time and end time for the game sessions, so we'll add those start times by matching the `player_id` and `game_session` with those of the `game_time` data set, which has start times for the game sessions, thereby adding the `game_start_time` and `game_end_time` for each game session.
Then we include only those game sessions that happened within 14 days of a participant taking the survey.

After that, we calculate the total time spend for each game mode and in total for each player.

Finally, we sum up all player stats for that period.

#load character dataset
```{r}
characters_raw <- read_both_waves("OII_PVZ_Character")
glimpse(characters_raw)
```

```{r}
characters_2week <- characters_raw %>%
  left_join(
    game_time_2week %>% 
      select(player_id, game_session, game_start_time, game_end_time),
    by = c("player_id", "game_session")
  ) %>%
  left_join(select(survey, player_id, start_date)) %>% 
  filter(game_start_time >= (start_date - days(14)))

```

```{r}
summary(characters_2week)
```



# Here we merge game time with the characters data set in order to be able to calculate game time per game mode propperly 
(the social hub games are sometimes pvp games, and the only way to distuingish between them is not by level key but to see if there were points scored)

This means we first need to create the characters data set and link it to the game time 2 week data set.
After that we can conitnue with calculating game time per game mode and total game time per player(per mode)


## game time per game mode


```{r}
# this shows that to identofy unique maps oyu need to look at "game_level_key"
unique(characters_2week[c("game_level")])
unique(characters_2week[c("game_level_key")])
```


Explanation:

There are several gamemodes (Social, PvE, Coop, PVP):

Neighbourville = Social HUB
- Dave manor (plant)
- Zomboss HQ (zombies)
- Giddy Park (combat area) This one has seasonal festivals that chance the map an and name of the map.

Free Roam Reggion = PvE ( story missions, questing, boss fights, collecting chests & gnomes, etc.)

Town Center:
- suburban town of Sundrop Hills, 
- bustling town of Up-Down-Uptown, 
- beachside port of Pressure Pier.

Mount Steep:
- western town of Olde Town, 
- desert cheese factory of Rocky Flats
- underground Steep Mines

Weirding Woods:
- Camp Near-a-Lake
- overgrown ruins of Z-Tech Factory
- Stirring Swamp.

Garden Ops = Coop game mode (team of 4 vs PvE)

Graveyard Ops = Coop game mode (team of 4 vs PvE)

Turf Takeover = PVP
A game mode where players must either capture or defend several objectives across an expanding map, which including either capturing/defending a point or escorting/stopping a payload. Plants and Zombies will be rotated to either capture or defend depending on the current map.

Team Vanquish = PVP
A team deathmatch, the first team to reach 50 vanquishes wins, however reviving a teammate takes one point off the enemy team.


a reasons to use game_level_key is because certain game_level_keys have NA as the game_level (e.g dsub_rush_resort has NA)

Q: how many individual games where analysed? 32596 matches from 490 players
I: USing the level of analyses, this goes beyond genres, games or feautures and looks at people's play behaviour

```{r}
# create new variables for type of game sessions


# social_hub
characters_2week$social_hub <- 0 #make all social hub
characters_2week$social_hub[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_social_terrain_summer" & is.na(characters_2week$score) ] <- 1 # code 0 the ones where there is performance data (score can only be gotten in pvp)


#social_session
#characters_2week$social_session <- 0

#characters_2week$social_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_social_terrain_beach"] <- 1 
#characters_2week$social_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_social_terrain_summer"] <- 1 
#characters_2week$social_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_socialspace"] <- 1 

#pve_session
characters_2week$pve_session <- 0

characters_2week$pve_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_region_cheesemines"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pve_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_region_dreadwood"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pve_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_region_towncenter"] <- 1 

#pvp_session
characters_2week$pvp_session <- 0

characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_dreadwood"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_towncenter"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_cheesemines"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_zrefinery"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_suburbs"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_dreadwood"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_farm"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_cheesemines"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_shoreline"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_resort"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_4v4_battlearena_v2"] <- 1 
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_4v4_superarena"] <- 1 
#this next line includes giddy park in PVP
characters_2week$pvp_session[characters_2week$game_level_key=="dsub_social_terrain_summer" & characters_2week$score == is.na(characters_2week$score) ] <- 1
```

Descriptive stats of new variables
```{r}

oii.freq(characters_2week$social_hub) 
# there are 7489  social hub sessions which is 28.24% of the games

oii.freq(characters_2week$pve_session) 
# there are 4854 pve sessions which is 18.31% of the games

oii.freq(characters_2week$pvp_session) 
# there are 13594 pvp sessions which is 51.27% of the games

```

# there are 9010 games without performance telemetry
On closer inspection these are matches that happened in the social hub, which is an idle space to navigate between game modes and customize oyur character.
No gameplay actually happens here so these will be removed. ALso some of these games 

```{r}
# drop the 9010 cases with no performance telemetry
characters_2week_nona <- characters_2week %>% drop_na(total_kill_count:shots_hit)
```

```{r}
oii.summary(characters_2week_nona)
# 17505 total matches with telemetry

oii.freq(characters_2week_nona$social_hub) 
# there are 0  social hub sessions which is 0% of the games

oii.freq(characters_2week_nona$pve_session) 
# there are 4509 pve sessions which is 25.76% of the games

oii.freq(characters_2week_nona$pvp_session) 
# there are 12445  pvp sessions which is 71.09% of the games
```

# total game time and game time per mode
```{r}
# then create separate "hours" played per player variables for each mode and total 

#total
game_time_players_total <- characters_2week %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(Seconds = sum(Seconds)) %>%
  mutate(Hours = Seconds/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#PVP
game_time_players_pvp <- characters_2week[characters_2week$pvp_session == "1", ] %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(pvp_Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(pvp_Seconds = sum(pvp_Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(pvp_Hours = pvp_Seconds/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#PVE
game_time_players_pve <- characters_2week[characters_2week$pve_session == "1", ] %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(pve_Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(pve_Seconds = sum(pve_Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(pve_Hours = pve_Seconds/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#social Hub
game_time_players_social <- characters_2week[characters_2week$social_hub == "1", ] %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(social_Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(social_Seconds = sum(social_Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(social_Hours = social_Seconds/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#total (NO NA (no social))
game_time_players_total_nona <- characters_2week_nona %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(Seconds_nona = sum(Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(Hours_nona = Seconds_nona/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#PVP (NO NA (no social))
game_time_players_pvp_nona <- characters_2week_nona[characters_2week_nona$pvp_session == "1", ] %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(pvp_Seconds_nona = sum(Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(pvp_Hours_nona = pvp_Seconds_nona/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#PVE (NO NA (no social))
game_time_players_pve_nona <- characters_2week_nona[characters_2week_nona$pve_session == "1", ] %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(pve_Seconds_nona = sum(Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(pve_Hours_nona = pve_Seconds_nona/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

#social Hub
game_time_players_social_nona <- characters_2week_nona[characters_2week_nona$social_hub == "1", ] %>% 
  mutate(duration = game_end_time-game_start_time) %>% 
  mutate(Seconds = as.numeric(duration)) %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(social_Seconds_nona = sum(Seconds)) %>% 
  mutate(social_Hours_nona = social_Seconds_nona/60/60) %>% 
  ungroup()

```

```{r}
#merge into game time players
game_time_players <- reduce(
  list(game_time_players_total, game_time_players_social, game_time_players_pve, game_time_players_pvp, game_time_players_total_nona, game_time_players_pve_nona, game_time_players_pvp_nona, game_time_players_social_nona),
  left_join)

game_time_players[is.na(game_time_players)] <- 0

game_time_players_nona <- reduce(
  list(game_time_players_total_nona, game_time_players_pve_nona, game_time_players_pvp_nona, game_time_players_social_nona),
  left_join)

game_time_players_nona[is.na(game_time_players_nona)] <- 0


```

```{r}
oii.summary(game_time_players)
```
```{r}
oii.summary(game_time_players_nona)
```

```{r}
characters_players <- characters_2week %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  ungroup()

characters_players_nona <- characters_2week_nona %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  ungroup()

```

### Game sessions & Game sessions per game mode

## calculate total Game sessions per player

1. calculate total game sessions
- How many games-sessions each player had, however this includes sessions in the hub (social) as well as pve and pvp.
```{r}
game_sessions_total <- characters_2week %>% 
  count(player_id)

game_sessions_total <- 
  rename(game_sessions_total, n_total_game_sessions = n)

glimpse(game_sessions_total)

game_sessions_total_nona <- characters_2week_nona %>% 
  count(player_id)

game_sessions_total_nona <- 
  rename(game_sessions_total_nona, n_total_game_sessions_nona = n)

glimpse(game_sessions_total_nona)
```


```{r}
oii.freq(characters_2week$game_level_key) 
```

```{r}
oii.freq(characters_2week_nona$game_level_key) 
```


## calculate total game sessions per game type
- Differentiating between social hub, giddy park, pve, pvp.

```{r}

# this is the correct one
game_sessions <- characters_2week %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(social_hub:pvp_session, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  ungroup()

glimpse(game_sessions)

game_sessions_nona <- characters_2week_nona %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(social_hub:pvp_session, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  ungroup()

glimpse(game_sessions_nona)
```

### Experience Points

This data set shows how many XP a player earned at what time and in what session.
The variables:

* `player_id` = Unique identifier for a player.
* `platform` = One of the major platforms, eg. PC, Playstation, Xbox.
* `date_time` = The date time when the xp was earned.
* `character_class` = The character key noting the character used.
* `game_session` = Unique identifier for a game session.
* `xp_earned` = The xp amount earned.

```{r}
library(anytime)
```

```{r cache = TRUE}
# Gotta process dates because they are different across the two files
xp_raw <- read_both_waves(
  "OII_PVZ_XPEarned", 
  col_types = cols(date_time = "c")
)

# All other timestamps, above, are automatically parsed to UTC so we do that here as well

xp <- xp_raw %>% 
  mutate(date_time = anytime(date_time, "UTC"))
glimpse(xp)
#write_rds(xp, here("data/ea/xp.rds"))
```

We sum up how many experience points a player got in total during the two weeks before the survey.

```{r}
xp_2weeks <- xp %>% 
  left_join(select(survey, player_id, start_date)) %>% 
  filter(date_time >= (start_date - days(14)))

```

```{r}
xp_players <- xp_2weeks %>% 
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(xp = sum(xp_earned)) %>% 
  ungroup()
```


### Calculate telemetry per game mode  
Making prefixes of pvp_ pve_

Hub is excluded because it had no telemetry


```{r}

# social hub  --> but is irrelevant because no telemetry
#social_hub_telemetry <- characters_2week[characters_2week$social_hub == "1", ] %>%
#  group_by(player_id) %>% 
#  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = FALSE)) %>%
#  setNames(paste0('hub_', names(.))) %>%
#  rename(player_id = hub_player_id)


```

```{r}
# pvp session
pvp_telemetry <- characters_2week[characters_2week$pvp_session == "1", ] %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  setNames(paste0('pvp_', names(.))) %>%
  rename(player_id = pvp_player_id)

pvp_telemetry_nona <- characters_2week_nona[characters_2week_nona$pvp_session == "1", ] %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  setNames(paste0('pvp_', names(.))) %>%
  rename(player_id = pvp_player_id)

```

```{r}
# giddy park
#giddy_park_telemetry <- characters_2week[characters_2week$giddy_park == "1", ] %>%
#  group_by(player_id) %>% 
# summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = FALSE)) %>%
#  setNames(paste0('giddy_', names(.))) %>%
#  rename(player_id = giddy_player_id)

```


```{r}
# pve session
pve_telemetry <- characters_2week[characters_2week$pve_session == "1", ] %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  setNames(paste0('pve_', names(.))) %>%
  rename(player_id = pve_player_id)

pve_telemetry_nona <- characters_2week_nona[characters_2week_nona$pve_session == "1", ] %>%
  group_by(player_id) %>% 
  summarise(across(total_kill_count:shots_hit, sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  setNames(paste0('pve_', names(.))) %>%
  rename(player_id = pve_player_id)

```


## Merge telemetry

```{r}
telemetry_players <- reduce(
  list(authentications_players, characters_players, game_time_players, xp_players, game_sessions, game_sessions_total, pvp_telemetry, pve_telemetry),
  left_join)

telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>% drop_na(total_kill_count:shots_hit)
telemetry_players[is.na(telemetry_players)] = 0

telemetry_players_nona <- reduce(
  list(authentications_players, characters_players_nona, game_time_players_nona, xp_players, game_sessions_nona, game_sessions_total_nona, pvp_telemetry_nona, pve_telemetry_nona),
  left_join)

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>% drop_na(total_kill_count:shots_hit)
telemetry_players_nona[is.na(telemetry_players_nona)] = 0
```


**Part 3: re-coding telemetry variables**


Variable ideas:
- KDA (kill count /death count)
- Hit ratio (shots hit/ shots fired)
- Critical hit ratio (crit hit count/shots hit)
- damage per game (total damage/total n games)
- experience per game (total exp/ total n games)



continue with variables creation, write something about it (JUSTIFY THEM)


Player-based measures (individual skill)
•	K/D ratio
•	Hit percentage (ratio of shots hit and shots fired)
•	Critical hit percentage
• DPS

Game-based measures (game performance)
•	average score per game
•	average damage per game


## K/D ratio
This is calculated per game mode becasue it is easier to kill in PVE then it is in PVP

```{r}
#K/D ratio

# a measure of in-game individual skill
# can have a high K/D but still lose the game because not focused on objectives

# all 0's in total death count will be calculated to 1 in order to calculate k/d and avoid  NaN (0/0), and inf (1/0). this is common. In a way it becomes kills per life, instead of kills per death 

telemetry_players$total_death_count[telemetry_players$total_death_count == 0] <- 1
telemetry_players$pve_total_death_count[telemetry_players$pve_total_death_count == 0] <- 1 
telemetry_players$pvp_total_death_count[telemetry_players$pvp_total_death_count == 0] <- 1

telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_kd = total_kill_count / total_death_count) %>%
  mutate(pve_kd = pve_total_kill_count / pve_total_death_count) %>%
  mutate(pvp_kd = pvp_total_kill_count / pvp_total_death_count)


# do same for nona list
telemetry_players_nona$total_death_count[telemetry_players_nona$total_death_count == 0] <- 1
telemetry_players_nona$pve_total_death_count[telemetry_players_nona$pve_total_death_count == 0] <- 1 
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_total_death_count[telemetry_players_nona$pvp_total_death_count == 0] <- 1

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_kd = total_kill_count / total_death_count) %>%
  mutate(pve_kd = pve_total_kill_count / pve_total_death_count) %>%
  mutate(pvp_kd = pvp_total_kill_count / pvp_total_death_count)

# in order to calculate K/D we have to deal with NA (NA/NA), NaN (0/0), and inf (60/0)

# NA (NA/NA)
# these stay and will be disregarded these had no telemtry, so person did not ploay that mode.

# NaN (0/0)
# this is a result of a player not dying or killing
# make the deaths 1 0/1

```

## hit accuracy 

```{r}
# hit accuracy 

# a measure of in-game skill that differs between PVP and PVE, it is easier to hit shots on mobs then people

telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_hit_accuracy = shots_hit / shots_fired) %>%
  mutate(pve_hit_accuracy = pve_shots_hit / pve_shots_fired) %>%
  mutate(pvp_hit_accuracy = pvp_shots_hit / pvp_shots_fired)

telemetry_players$total_hit_accuracy[is.nan(telemetry_players$total_hit_accuracy)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pve_hit_accuracy[is.nan(telemetry_players$pve_hit_accuracy)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pvp_hit_accuracy[is.nan(telemetry_players$pvp_hit_accuracy)] <- 0

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_hit_accuracy = shots_hit / shots_fired) %>%
  mutate(pve_hit_accuracy = pve_shots_hit / pve_shots_fired) %>%
  mutate(pvp_hit_accuracy = pvp_shots_hit / pvp_shots_fired)

telemetry_players_nona$total_hit_accuracy[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$total_hit_accuracy)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pve_hit_accuracy[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pve_hit_accuracy)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_hit_accuracy[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pvp_hit_accuracy)] <- 0

# have to deal with NA (NA/NA), NaN (0/0), and 0.000 (0 hits)

# NA's are player who did not have telemetry, so person did not ploay that mode.
#0 are players who did not shoot 
```

## critical hit ratio

```{r}
# critical hit ratio

# a measure of in-game skill

telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_crit_ratio = critical_hit_count / shots_hit) %>%
  mutate(pve_crit_ratio = pve_critical_hit_count / pve_shots_hit) %>%
  mutate(pvp_crit_ratio = pvp_critical_hit_count / pvp_shots_hit) 

# have to deal with NA (NA/NA), NaN (0/0), and 0.000 (0 crits)
telemetry_players$total_crit_ratio[is.nan(telemetry_players$total_crit_ratio)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pve_crit_ratio[is.nan(telemetry_players$pve_crit_ratio)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pvp_crit_ratio[is.nan(telemetry_players$pvp_crit_ratio)] <- 0

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_crit_ratio = critical_hit_count / shots_hit) %>%
  mutate(pve_crit_ratio = pve_critical_hit_count / pve_shots_hit) %>%
  mutate(pvp_crit_ratio = pvp_critical_hit_count / pvp_shots_hit) 

# have to deal with NA (NA/NA), NaN (0/0), and 0.000 (0 crits)
telemetry_players_nona$total_crit_ratio[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$total_crit_ratio)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pve_crit_ratio[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pve_crit_ratio)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_crit_ratio[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pvp_crit_ratio)] <- 0



# NA's are player who did not have telemetry, so person did not ploay that mode.
#0 are players who did not shoot 
```


## per game variables 
I can create variables of performance over total games, but this includes all game types so is pretty uninformative
I first need to create 
  - total damage per gametype
  - total score per game type
  - total xp per game type
  
to do this i need to use the raw chacters data for damage and score

 
## damage per game (average damage dealt)
```{r}
#damage per game 

# create an avareage damaage per game by dividing it with total matches
telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_average_damage = damage_dealt / n_total_game_sessions) %>%
  mutate(pve_average_damage = pve_damage_dealt / pve_session) %>%
  mutate(pvp_average_damage = pvp_damage_dealt / pvp_session) 

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_average_damage = damage_dealt / n_total_game_sessions_nona) %>%
  mutate(pve_average_damage = pve_damage_dealt / pve_session) %>%
  mutate(pvp_average_damage = pvp_damage_dealt / pvp_session) 

# NA is when no telemtry was available, so person did not ploay that mode.
telemetry_players$total_average_damage[is.nan(telemetry_players$total_average_damage)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pve_average_damage[is.nan(telemetry_players$pve_average_damage)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pvp_average_damage[is.nan(telemetry_players$pvp_average_damage)] <- 0

telemetry_players_nona$total_average_damage[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$total_average_damage)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pve_average_damage[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pve_average_damage)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_average_damage[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pvp_average_damage)] <- 0

```

## damage per second
```{r}
#damage per second


# create an average damage per second by dividing it with total seconds
telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_dps = damage_dealt / Seconds) %>%
  mutate(pve_dps = pve_damage_dealt / pve_Seconds) %>%
  mutate(pvp_dps = pvp_damage_dealt / pvp_Seconds) 

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_dps = damage_dealt / Seconds_nona) %>%
  mutate(pve_dps = pve_damage_dealt / pve_Seconds_nona) %>%
  mutate(pvp_dps = pvp_damage_dealt / pvp_Seconds_nona) 

# NA is when no telemtry was available, so person did not ploay that mode.
telemetry_players$total_dps[is.nan(telemetry_players$total_dps)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pve_dps[is.nan(telemetry_players$pve_dps)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pvp_dps[is.nan(telemetry_players$pvp_dps)] <- 0

telemetry_players_nona$total_dps[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$total_dps)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pve_dps[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pve_dps)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_dps[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pvp_dps)] <- 0


```

## score per game (average score)
```{r}
#score per game

telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_average_score = score / n_total_game_sessions) %>%
  mutate(pve_average_score = pve_score / pve_session) %>%
  mutate(pvp_average_score = pvp_score / pvp_session)

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_average_score = score / n_total_game_sessions_nona) %>%
  mutate(pve_average_score = pve_score / pve_session) %>%
  mutate(pvp_average_score = pvp_score / pvp_session)

# NA is when no telemtry was available, so person did not ploay that mode.
telemetry_players$total_average_score[is.nan(telemetry_players$total_average_score)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pve_average_score[is.nan(telemetry_players$pve_average_score)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pvp_average_score[is.nan(telemetry_players$pvp_average_score)] <- 0

telemetry_players_nona$total_average_score[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$total_average_score)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pve_average_score[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pve_average_score)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_average_score[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pvp_average_score)] <- 0

```

## score per second
```{r}
#score per second

telemetry_players <- telemetry_players %>%
  mutate(total_sps = score / Seconds) %>%
  mutate(pve_sps = pve_score / pve_Seconds) %>%
  mutate(pvp_sps = pvp_score / pvp_Seconds)

telemetry_players_nona <- telemetry_players_nona %>%
  mutate(total_sps = score / Seconds_nona) %>%
  mutate(pve_sps = pve_score / pve_Seconds_nona) %>%
  mutate(pvp_sps = pvp_score / pvp_Seconds_nona)

# NA is when no telemtry was available, so person did not ploay that mode.
telemetry_players$total_sps[is.nan(telemetry_players$total_sps)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pve_sps[is.nan(telemetry_players$pve_sps)] <- 0
telemetry_players$pvp_sps[is.nan(telemetry_players$pvp_sps)] <- 0

telemetry_players_nona$total_sps[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$total_sps)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pve_sps[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pve_sps)] <- 0
telemetry_players_nona$pvp_sps[is.nan(telemetry_players_nona$pvp_sps)] <- 0

```


## XP per hour as a measure of progression
Maybe i will use this maybe not, depends if i want to look at progression.but it doesnt actually measure it well. a small action might be a use progression in th end game.
other things are level and prestige



**Part 4: merging telemetry_player and survey dataframes** 

```{r}
# Merge telemetry with survey
pvz_df <- survey %>% 
  right_join(telemetry_players, by = "player_id")

pvz_df_nona <- survey %>% 
  right_join(telemetry_players_nona, by = "player_id")
```




## create limited dataframe with relevant variables 
```{r}
# original frame has 433 obs with 124 variables


```


**Part 5: Descriptive statistics **
1. describe game sessions
2. describe players
3. describe total game session per player and hwo they differ


in methodology just name the total number of game sessions and say they are split between all elements of the game. the 2 week filter and split come in descriptives section, where we show this plot.


1. game session descriptives, because that is the raw format of the data  (name n of matches and n of variables)
2. player based descriptives, because the analysis is on player level ( name n of players and n of variables)



## 1. Describing game sessions

```{r}

```

Descriptive stats of new variables
```{r}

oii.freq(characters_2week$social_hub) 
# there are 7489  social hub sessions which is 28.24% of the games

oii.freq(characters_2week$pve_session) 
# there are 4854 pve sessions which is 18.31% of the games

oii.freq(characters_2week$pvp_session) 
# there are 13594 pvp sessions which is 51.27% of the games

oii.freq(game_time_2week$match_type) 

```

## 2. Desribing players

## Demographics

In total, 433 players responded to the survey and logged in in the last 2 weeks.
Their mean age was 35.4 with a standard deviation of 11.8. 337 were male (77.83%), and 82 female  (18.94), 1 other, and 13 prefered not to say.

However only 380 of those had actual performance telemetry available.

Still of the 380, 3 player played for less then a seconds and will be removed.After removal the player with the lowest playtime was 101 seconds

Mean age 35.394 with a 11.857
292  were male (77.25%), and 73 female(19.31) , 1 other, and 12 prefered not to say.

so no big difference (and this is also not different from the literature)

## age & gender

```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$age)
oii.freq(pvz_df$gender)
```
```{r}
# drop cases with 0 seconds playtime 
pvz_df_nona<-subset(pvz_df_nona, Seconds_nona >101)
```


## age & gender
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df_nona$age)
oii.freq(pvz_df_nona$gender)
```

## univariate statistics of the measures

## competence 
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$competence)
hist(pvz_df$competence)

```
Player-based measures (individual skill)
•	K/D ratio
•	Hit percentage (ratio of shots hit and shots fired)
•	Critical hit percentage

## kd 
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$total_kd)
hist(pvz_df$total_kd, breaks = 150)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
# hit percentage
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$total_hit_accuracy)
hist(pvz_df$total_hit_accuracy)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
# crit ratio
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$total_crit_ratio)
hist(pvz_df$total_crit_ratio)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
Game-based measures (game performance)
•	average score per game
•	average damage per game

```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$total_average_score)
hist(pvz_df$total_average_score)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
#average damage per game
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$total_average_damage)
hist(pvz_df$total_average_damage)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
# total games
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df$n_total_game_sessions)
hist(pvz_df$n_total_game_sessions)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
#pvp session
```{r}

oii.summary(pvz_df$pvp_session)
hist(pvz_df$pvp_session, breaks = 500
    )

# but this includes all modes of play
```
#pve session
```{r}

oii.summary(pvz_df$pve_session)
hist(pvz_df$pve_session, breaks = 350)

# but this includes all modes of play
```
#social hub
```{r}

oii.summary(pvz_df$social_hub)
hist(pvz_df$social_hub, breaks = 250)

# but this includes all modes of play
```

```{r}

```


## 3. Analysis of play behaviour during a session (they are consist of different match types)

# Convert date time 
```{r}
# calculate date time for game time raw
game_time_raw$game_start_time <- as.POSIXct(game_time_raw$game_start_time, format = "%y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
game_time_raw$game_end_time <- as.POSIXct(game_time_raw$game_end_time, format = "%y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
```   

```{r}
#convert date time into time only
game_time_raw$start_time_only <- format(as.POSIXct(game_time_raw$game_start_time,format='%y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'),format='%H:%M:%S')
game_time_raw$end_time_only <- format(as.POSIXct(game_time_raw$game_end_time,format='%y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'),format='%H:%M:%S')

```

# recode variables to colour-code matches
```{r}
# recode a new varible with PVP (Arena), PVP (vanquish), PVP (rush),PVE, SOCIAL_HUB 

# there are 18 maps, these maps all belong to seperate game modes, some game modes have more maps then others. To understand the play behaviour these maps will be grouped according to their respective game mode.

# An overal game sessions is split into smaller sessions of different game type, these will be calle matches for clarity. So, one game sessions consists of several distinct matches. 


game_time_raw$match_type <- 0

# social match 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_social_terrain_beach"] <- "social match" 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_social_terrain_summer"] <- "social match"  
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_socialspace"] <- "social match"  

#pve match
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_region_cheesemines"] <- "pve match" 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_region_dreadwood"] <- "pve match" 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_region_towncenter"] <- "pve session" 

#pvp match (Rush)

game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_dreadwood"] <- "pvp match (rush)" 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_towncenter"] <- "pvp match (rush)"  
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_farm"] <- "pvp match (rush)" 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_cheesemines"] <- "pvp match (rush)"  
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_shoreline"] <- "pvp match (rush)"  
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_rush_resort"] <- "pvp match (rush)" 

#pvp match (Vanquish)
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_cheesemines"] <- "pvp match (vanquish)"  
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_zrefinery"] <- "pvp match (vanquish)"
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_suburbs"] <- "pvp match (vanquish)" 
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_tvm_dreadwood"] <- "pvp match (vanquish)"

#pvp match (Arena)
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_4v4_battlearena_v2"] <- "pvp match (arena)"
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_4v4_superarena"] <- "pvp match (arena)"

#podium celebration
game_time_raw$match_type[game_time_raw$game_level_key=="dsub_podiumcelebration"] <- "podium celebration"
  
# it would be great it i can merge the frames so that i can include giddy park and social hub  
  
  
```

##order them
```{r}
telemetry_players[order(telemetry_players$n_total_game_sessions, decreasing = TRUE),]  
```

## create slices of 3 random players
```{r}
# choose cases (slices)
tmp1 <- telemetry_players %>% 
  slice(401) %>% 
  left_join(game_time_raw) %>%
  arrange(game_start_time, game_end_time)

tmp1$start_time_only <- as.POSIXct(tmp1$start_time_only, format = "%H:%M:%S")
tmp1$end_time_only <- as.POSIXct(tmp1$end_time_only, format = "%H:%M:%S")

tmp2 <- telemetry_players %>% 
  slice(6) %>% 
  left_join(game_time_raw) %>%
  arrange(game_start_time, game_end_time)

tmp2$start_time_only <- as.POSIXct(tmp2$start_time_only, format = "%H:%M:%S")
tmp2$end_time_only <- as.POSIXct(tmp2$end_time_only, format = "%H:%M:%S")

tmp3 <- telemetry_players %>% 
  slice(3) %>%   #436  #401
  left_join(game_time_raw) %>%
  arrange(game_start_time, game_end_time)

tmp3$start_time_only <- as.POSIXct(tmp3$start_time_only, format = "%H:%M:%S")
tmp3$end_time_only <- as.POSIXct(tmp3$end_time_only, format = "%H:%M:%S")


```


```{r}
#visualize the cases

plot1 <- tmp1 %>%   
  rowid_to_column() %>% 
  ggplot(aes(start_time_only, rowid, col = match_type)) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Match type", breaks = c("social match","pve match","pvp match (rush)","pvp match (vanquish)","pvp match (arena)","podium celebration", "0"),
                        values=c("#2ECC71", "#4E7FE3", "#E74C3C", "#F39C12", "#FFEE4B","#9B59B6","#9B59B6")) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = end_time_only, yend = rowid)) +
  scale_x_datetime(labels = date_format("%H:%M"),breaks = date_breaks("2 hours")) +
  xlab("Time (hours)") + ylab("Game matches") +
  theme(legend.position = "right", axis.text.y = element_blank(),axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), panel.background = element_blank(), axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"))

plot2 <- tmp2 %>%   
  rowid_to_column() %>% 
  ggplot(aes(start_time_only, rowid, col = match_type)) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Match type",breaks = c("social match","pve match","pvp match (rush)","pvp match (vanquish)","pvp match (arena)","podium celebration","0"),
                        values=c("#2ECC71", "#4E7FE3", "#E74C3C", "#F39C12", "#FFEE4B","#9B59B6","#9B59B6")) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = end_time_only, yend = rowid)) +
  scale_x_datetime(labels = date_format("%H:%M"),breaks = date_breaks("2 hours")) +
  xlab("") + ylab("Game matches") +
  theme(legend.position = "right", axis.text.y = element_blank(),axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), panel.background = element_blank(), axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"))


lims <- as.POSIXct(strptime(c("11:00", "23:59"), 
                   format = "%H:%M"))

plot3 <- tmp3 %>%   
  rowid_to_column() %>% 
  ggplot(aes(start_time_only, rowid, col = match_type)) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Match type",breaks = c("social match","pve match","pvp match (rush)","pvp match (vanquish)","pvp match (arena)","podium celebration","0"),
                        values=c("#2ECC71", "#4E7FE3", "#E74C3C", "#F39C12", "#FFEE4B","#9B59B6","#9B59B6")) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = end_time_only, yend = rowid)) +
  scale_x_datetime(labels = date_format("%H:%M"),breaks = date_breaks("2 hours"), 
                        limits = lims) +
  xlab("") + ylab("Game matches") +
  theme(legend.position = "right", axis.text.y = element_blank(),axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), panel.background = element_blank(), axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"))

#make a variable where date is gone and its just time. thats why the dot are so small
```

```{r}
#library(ggpubr)
figure <- ggarrange(plot2, plot3, plot1,
                    labels = c("Player A", "Player B", "Player C"),
                    ncol = 1, nrow = 3, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "right")
figure
```




**Part 6: analysis **
1. cluster groups
2. run regressions with all relevant variables



## 1.  clustering to find different groups of players based on differences in the game matches

Clustering is a technique of data segmentation that partitions the data into several groups based on their similarity. 

# instal packages 
```{r}
install.packages("stats") 
install.packages("ggplot2") 
install.packages("ggfortify") 
```

```{r}

library(stats)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggfortify)
library(factoextra)
```

## set NA's to 0
```{r}

pvz_df <- pvz_df %>%
  mutate_at(vars(giddy_park,social_hub, pve_session, pvp_session,n_total_game_sessions), ~replace_na(., 0))
```


## calculate ratios of game mode played
```{r}

pvz_df <- pvz_df %>%
  mutate(ratio_social_hub = social_hub / n_total_game_sessions) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pve_session = pve_session / n_total_game_sessions) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pvp_session = pvp_session / n_total_game_sessions)
```

```{r}

clusterdata <- pvz_df %>% 
  select(ratio_pve_session,ratio_pvp_session)
clusterdata[is.na(clusterdata)] <- 0
```


Create a function for a WSS plot to find the optimal number of clusters
```{r}
wssplot <- function(data, nc=15, seed=1234){
                  wss <- (nrow(data)-1)*sum(apply(data,2,var))
                      for (i in 2:nc){
                set.seed(seed)
                    wss[i] <- sum(kmeans(data, centers=i)$withinss)}
              plot(1:nc, wss, type="b", xlab="Number of Clusters",
                            ylab="Within groups sum of squares")
       }
```

```{r}
wssplot(clusterdata2)
```
The WSS plot shows that there are 3 or 4 optimal number of clusters

The data must be standardized (i.e., scaled) to make variables comparable.
```{r}
clusterdata <- scale(clusterdata)
```

Build the K-Means cluster analysis
```{r}
set.seed(100)
KM = kmeans(clusterdata, 3)
```

Looking at the cluster centers
```{r}

KM$centers

# cluster 1 == PVP
# cluster 2 == PVE
```

# assign the cluster as a variable to the df
```{r}
pvz_df$cluster <- KM$cluster
```


Evaluating the cluster analysis
```{r}
ggplot(pvz_df, aes(x=ratio_pve_session, y= ratio_pvp_session, col= as.factor(pvz_df$cluster))) + geom_point()
```



```{r}
library(scatterplot3d)
scatterplot3d(clusterdata, pch=20, color=rainbow(2)[KM$cluster])
```


```{r}
descriptives <- pvz_df_nona %>% 
  select(spane_game_balance,Hours,ratio_pvp_Hours,pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score,
         ratio_pve_Hours,pve_kd,pve_hit_accuracy,pve_dps,pve_average_damage,pve_average_score)
clusterdata2[is.na(clusterdata2)] <- 0
```
spane_game_balance,Hours,ratio_pvp_Hours,pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score,
         ratio_pve_Hours,pve_kd,pve_hit_accuracy,pve_dps,pve_average_damage,pve_average_score


```{r}
desc <- pvz_df_nona[,c("spane_game_balance","Hours","ratio_pvp_Hours","pvp_kd","pvp_hit_accuracy","pvp_dps","pvp_average_damage","pvp_average_score",
         "ratio_pve_Hours","pve_kd","pve_hit_accuracy","pve_dps","pve_average_damage","pve_average_score")]


```

```{r}

st(desc)

```



### do clusting based on game time

I am going to use k means clustering for this analysis. The cluster analysis comprises the following steps…

1. Assess clustering tendency of the data
2. Estimate number of clusters
3. Run Clustering Algorithm
4. Assess Validity of Clustering Results
5. Interpret clustering results

```{r}
#rename variables 

pvz_df_nona <- pvz_df_nona %>% 
  rename(
    Hours = Hours_nona,
    pvp_Hours = pvp_Hours_nona,
    pve_Hours = pve_Hours_nona,
    social_Hours = social_Hours_nona,
    )

```

## calculate ratios of game mode played
```{r}
pvz_df3 <- pvz_df_nona
```

## calculate ratios of game mode played
```{r}

pvz_df3 <- pvz_df3 %>%
  mutate(ratio_social_Hours = social_Hours / Hours) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pve_Hours = pve_Hours / Hours) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pvp_Hours = pvp_Hours / Hours)

```


## 1. assessing cluster tendency of the data VISUAL METHOD

```{r}
install.packages("clustertend")
```

```{r}
library(clustertend)
```

Make two datasets, the one used and a random data set generated from that one
```{r}
# data set
clusterdata1 <- pvz_df3 %>% 
  select(Hours,ratio_pvp_Hours,pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score,
         ratio_pve_Hours,pve_kd,pve_hit_accuracy,pve_dps,pve_average_damage,pve_average_score)
clusterdata2[is.na(clusterdata2)] <- 0

# Random data generated from cluster data
random_df <- apply(clusterdata2, 2,
function(x){runif(length(x), min(x), (max(x)))})
random_df <- as.data.frame(random_df)

# Standardize the data sets
clusterdata2 <- scale(clusterdata1)
random_df <- scale(random_df)
```

```{r}
# Plot faithful data set
fviz_pca_ind(prcomp(clusterdata2), title = "PCA - performance data",
geom = "point", ggtheme = theme_classic(),
legend = "bottom")

# can add for colour map: habillage = pvz_df3$cluster,

# Plot the random df
fviz_pca_ind(prcomp(random_df), title = "PCA - Random data",
geom = "point", ggtheme = theme_classic())
```
It can be seen that the performance data set contains 4 real clusters. However the randomly
generated data set doesn’t contain any meaningful clusters.


## statistical cluster tendency test (Hopkins test)
The Hopkins statistic is used to assess the clustering tendency of a data set by
measuring the probability that a given data set is generated by a uniform data
distribution. In other words, it tests the spatial randomness of the data.

```{r}
# Compute Hopkins statistic for iris dataset
set.seed(123)
hopkins(clusterdata2, n = nrow(clusterdata2)-1)

dist.cor<-get_dist(clusterdata2, method = "pearson")
dcor1 <-fviz_dist(dist.cor, show_labels = FALSE, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07"))

plot(dcor1)

# tot calculate the hopkins you have to do 1- 0.1213977 = 0.8786023
# see: https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/332651/validating-cluster-tendency-using-hopkins-statistic
```
```{r}
# Compute Hopkins statistic for iris dataset
```


```{r}
# Compute Hopkins statistic for a random dataset
set.seed(123)
hopkins(random_df, n = nrow(random_df)-1)

dist.cor2<-get_dist(random_df, method = "pearson")
fviz_dist(dist.cor2, show_labels = FALSE, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07"))
```
• Null hypothesis: the data set D is uniformly distributed (i.e., no meaningful clusters)
• Alternative hypothesis: the data set D is not uniformly distributed (i.e., contains meaningful clusters)

It can be seen that the performance data set is highly clusterable (the H value = 0.11 which
is far below the threshold 0.5). However the random_df data set is not clusterable
(H = 0.50)

This is indeed highly clusterable, the H for the Iris data set (a famous example dataset for clustering) is 0.18.

The result of the Hopkins Test is 0.1213977 which is well below the 0.5 threshold and therefore indicates the data is clusterable.
0.502174
We can also use a visual method to assess clustering tendency. Visual Assessment of Clustering Tendency works by computing the dissimilarity matrix of objects in the data set (using Euclidean distance). Reorder the matrix so that similar objects are close to one another.


## 2.  determining the optimal number of clusters

Elbow method:

the basic idea behind partitioning methods, such as k-means clustering
(Chapter 4), is to define clusters such that the total intra-cluster variation [or total
within-cluster sum of square (WSS)] is minimized. The total WSS measures the
compactness of the clustering and we want it to be as small as possible.
The Elbow method looks at the total WSS as a function of the number of clusters:
One should choose a number of clusters so that adding another cluster doesn’t improve
much better the total WSS.

Create a function for a WSS plot to find the optimal number of clusters
```{r}
wssplot <- function(data, nc=15, seed=1234){
                  wss <- (nrow(data)-1)*sum(apply(data,2,var))
                      for (i in 2:nc){
                set.seed(seed)
                    wss[i] <- sum(kmeans(data, centers=i)$withinss)}
              plot(1:nc, wss, type="b", xlab="Number of Clusters",
                            ylab="Within groups sum of squares")
       }
```

```{r}
set.seed(123)
fviz_nbclust(clusterdata2, kmeans, method = "wss") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5, linetype = 2)+
labs(title = "")
```
The plot above represents the variance within the clusters. It decreases as k increases,
but it can be seen a bend (or “elbow”) at k = 5. This bend indicates that additional
clusters beyond the fourth have little value.. In the next section, we’ll classify the
observations into 5 clusters.

The WSSs (within sum of square) plot shows that there are 5 optimal number of clusters

```{r}
install.packages("NbClust")
```

```{r}
library(NbClust)
```

```{r}
# Gap statistic
# nboot = 50 to keep the function speedy.
# recommended value: nboot= 500 for your analysis.
# Use verbose = FALSE to hide computing progression.
set.seed(123)
fviz_nbclust(clusterdata2, kmeans, method = "gap_stat", nboot = 500)+
labs(title = "")
```
```{r}
nb <- NbClust(clusterdata2, distance = "euclidean", min.nc = 2,
max.nc = 10, method = "kmeans")
```

```{r}
fviz_nbclust(nb)
```

## 3. Run clustering algorithm and visualize

Build the K-Means cluster analysis
```{r}
#compute kmeans
set.seed(100)
km.random <- eclust(random_df, "kmeans", k = 5,
                 nstart = 50, graph = FALSE)

# K-means clustering
km.res <- eclust(clusterdata2, "kmeans", k = 5,
                 nstart = 50, graph = FALSE)


# visualize
fviz_cluster(km.res, data = clusterdata2,
             geom = c("point"),
             show.clust.cent = TRUE,
             ellipse = TRUE,
             ellipse.type = "convex",
             outlier.color = "black",
             ggtheme = theme_classic(),
             main = "Player clusters")

# visualize
fviz_cluster(km.random, data = random_df,
             geom = c("point"),
             show.clust.cent = TRUE,
             ellipse = TRUE,
             ellipse.type = "convex",
             outlier.color = "black",
             ggtheme = theme_classic(),
             main = "Random clusters")
```

## 3. Run clustering algorithm and visualize

Build the K-Means cluster analysis
```{r}
#compute kmeans
set.seed(100)
km.random <- eclust(random_df, "kmeans", k = 5,
                 nstart = 50, graph = FALSE)

# K-means clustering
km.res <- eclust(clusterdata2, "kmeans", k = 5,
                 nstart = 50, graph = FALSE)


# visualize
pp<-fviz_cluster(km.res, data = clusterdata2,
             palette = "Set2",
             geom = c("point"),
             show.clust.cent = TRUE,
             ellipse = TRUE,
             ellipse.type = "convex",
             outlier.color = "black",
             ggtheme = theme_classic(),
             main = "Player clusters")

# visualize
fviz_cluster(km.random, data = random_df,
             palette = "Set2",
             geom = c("point"),
             show.clust.cent = TRUE,
             ellipse = TRUE,
             ellipse.type = "convex",
             outlier.color = "black",
             ggtheme = theme_classic(),
             main = "Random clusters")

pp
```


```{r}

```

### cluster validation 
The term cluster validation is used to design the procedure of evaluating the
goodness of clustering algorithm results. This is important to avoid finding patterns in
a random data, as well as, in the situation where you want to compare two clustering
algorithms.

## internal validation  
Internal cluster validation, which uses the internal information of the clustering process to evaluate the goodness of a clustering structure without reference
to external information. It can be also used for estimating the number of clusters
and the appropriate clustering algorithm without any external data.

external validation is not possible because there are no externally known results.

Internal validation measures reflect often the compactness, the connectedness and
the separation of the cluster 

In this section, we’ll describe the two commonly used indices for assessing the
goodness of clustering: the silhouette width and the Dunn index. These internal
measure can be used also to determine the optimal number of clusters in the data.

# silhouette plot
To confirm that your number of classes is indeed optimal, there is a way to evaluate the quality of your clustering via the silhouette plot (which shows the silhouette coefficient on the y axis).

```{r}
fviz_silhouette(km.res,palette = "Set2")

# the Mean silhouette width (MSW) is positive so the classification is strong.
```
```{r}

set.seed(100)
# K-means clustering
km.res4 <- eclust(clusterdata2, "kmeans", k = 5,
                 nstart = 50, graph = FALSE)

fviz_silhouette(km.res4,palette = "Set2")

# the Mean silhouette width (MSW) is positive so the classification is strong.
```



As a reminder, the interpretation of the silhouette coefficient is as follows:

> 0 means that the observation is well grouped. The closer the coefficient is to 1, the better the observation is grouped.
< 0 means that the observation has been placed in the wrong cluster.
= 0 means that the observation is between two clusters.

It can be seen that several samples have a negative silhouette coefficient in the kmeans clustering. This means that they are not in the right cluster.

We can find the name of these samples and determine the clusters they are closer (neighbor cluster), as follow:

several other variations (k = 4, 3, 6) where tried but the ASW didnt improve and some observations always stayed below 0. Reassiging these to the new clusters, created new clusters with other samples below 0. Still the AWS is > 0 so the observations are well grouped and the clustering is validated.


# now create new clusters and compare the old with the new with dunn index, the one with the higher dunn index is the better cluster
```{r}
# Silhouette width of observation
sil <- km.res$silinfo$widths[, 1:3]

```

```{r}
# Objects with negative silhouette
neg_sil_index <- which(sil[, 'sil_width'] < 0)

sil[neg_sil_index, , drop = FALSE]
```
There are 34 cases that are in the wrong cluster and should be switched to the closest neighbor
```{r}
# Replace clusters with negative silhouette
km.res2 <- transform(km.res$silinfo$widths, cluster = ifelse(sil_width < 0, neighbor, cluster))

km.res3
```

```{r}

km.res3 <- transform(km.res$silinfo$widths, cluster = ifelse(sil_width < 0, neighbor, cluster))
```
transform(sil, cluster = ifelse(sil_width < 0, sil_neighbour, cluster))



#create new df with sil data
```{r}
sil2 <- cbind(ID = rownames(sil), sil)
rownames(sil) <- 1:nrow(sil)
```
# make sure they are ordered on ID
```{r}
sil2$neighbor= as.numeric(as.character(sil2$neighbor))
sil2$sil_width = as.numeric(as.character(sil2$sil_width))

sil2$ID = as.numeric(as.character(sil2$ID))
sil2 <- sil2[order(sil2$ID),]
```
# create new variables in pvz_df_nona
```{r}
pvz_df_nona$sil_cluster <- sil2$cluster
pvz_df_nona$sil_neighbour <- sil2$neighbor
pvz_df_nona$sil_width <- sil2$sil_width
```

# change the 34 cases where sil_width is negative 
```{r}
pvz_df_nona_3 <- transform(pvz_df_nona, cluster = ifelse(sil_width < 0, sil_neighbour, cluster))
```




## do clustering again with new df (_3) and compare (clusters, sil and dunn index)


# Dunn index
The Dunn index is a internal clustering validation measure.

If the data set contains compact and well-separated clusters, the diameter of the
clusters is expected to be small and the distance between the clusters is expected to
be large. Thus, Dunn index should be maximized.

```{r}
install.packages("fpc")
library(fpc)
```

```{r}
# Statistics for k-means clustering
km_stats <- cluster.stats(dist(clusterdata2), km.res$cluster)
# Dun index
km_stats$dunn
```






## 5.  Final cluster interpretation

Looking at the cluster centers
```{r}
km.res$centers
```

```{r}
table1 <-aggregate(clusterdata1, by=list(cluster=km.res$cluster), mean)
table1
```


## visualize table
```{r}
install.packages("formattable")
library(formattable)
```


```{r}
formattable(table1, 
            align =c("l","c","c","c","c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c"), 
            list(`cluster` = formatter(
              "span", style = ~ style(color = "grey",font.weight = "bold")),
              `Hours` = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'ratio_pvp_Hours' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pvp_kd' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pvp_hit_accuracy' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pvp_crit_ratio' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pvp_dps' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pvp_average_damage' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pvp_average_score' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'ratio_pve_Hours' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pve_kd' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pve_hit_accuracy' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pve_crit_ratio' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pve_dps' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pve_average_damage' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97"),
              'pve_average_score' = color_tile("#FDFEFD","#71CA97")
))
```

# cluster 1 == 1st in Hours, 3rd PVP ratio, 3rd pvp kd, 3rd pvp accuracy, 3rd pvp dps, 3rd average damage, 4th pvp score, 3rd pve ratio, 4th pve kd, 4th pve hit accuracy, 4th pve dps, 4th pve average damage, 4th pve average score

Casual players: Play the most by far with an even split between PVP and PVE content. Below average perforamnce in both game modes


# cluster 2 ==  5th in hours, 1st PVP ratio, 4th pvp kd, 4th pvp accuracy, 4th pvp dps, 4th average damage, 3rd pvp score, 5th pve ratio, 5th pve kd, 5th pve hit accuracy, 5th pve dps, 5th pve average damage, 5th pve average score

PVP Noobs: Lowest playtime: only play pvp and almost no pve. Below average performance in PVP and consistently the worst performance in PVE.

# cluster 3 == 4th in Hours, 5th PVP ratio, 5th pvp kd, 5th pvp accuracy,  5th pvp dps, 5th pvp score, 1st pve ratio, 3rd pve kd, 3rd pve hit accuracy,  3rd pve dps, 3rd pve average damage, 2nd pve average score

PVE adventurers: Below average playtime, play the least PVP and the most PVE, performance in PVP is the worst of all players, PVE performance is average.

# cluster 4 ==  3rd in Hours, 4th PVP ratio, 1st pvp kd, 1st pvp accuracy,  1st pvp dps, 1st pvp score, 2nd pve ratio, 2nd pve kd, 2nd pve hit accuracy,  2nd pve dps, 2nd pve average damage, 3rd pve average score

ex-PVP Elite (Veterans): play below average amounts, play little PVP, but when they do they perform the best of all. When they play PVE they perform above avearge.

# cluster 5 == 2nd in Hours, 2nd PVP ratio, 2nd pvp kd, 2nd pvp accuracy, 2nd pvp dps, 2nd pvp score, 4th pve ratio, 1st pve kd, 1st pve hit accuracy, 1st pve dps, 1st pve average damage, 1st pve average score

Elite : second most playtime, play more pvp then pve and are among the best in both (second best in PVP and the best in PVE)



# assign cluter to data frame for analysis
```{r}
pvz_df3$cluster <- km.res$cluster
pvz_df_nona$cluster <- km.res$cluster

```

```{r}
oii.freq(pvz_df_nona$cluster)
```





## univariate
for Hours
for Cluster
for Wellbeing


## fucking around with normal regressions

select(Hours,ratio_pvp_Hours,pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_crit_ratio,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score,
         ratio_pve_Hours,pve_kd,pve_hit_accuracy,pve_crit_ratio,pve_dps,pve_average_damage,pve_average_score)
         
pvz_df3 <- pvz_df3 %>%
  mutate(ratio_social_Hours = social_Hours / Hours) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pve_Hours = pve_Hours / Hours) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pvp_Hours = pvp_Hours / Hours)
```{r}
pvz_df_nona <- pvz_df_nona %>%
  mutate(ratio_social_Hours = social_Hours / Hours) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pve_Hours = pve_Hours / Hours) %>%
  mutate(ratio_pvp_Hours = pvp_Hours / Hours)
```


# make latent variables for performance
either use PCA
or 
rescale them into standardized units (dividing by the STD and subtracting the mean) --> this gives you new variables with a mean of 0 and an STD of 1 and then you can combine them together. The variables have to be conceptually similar.
```{r}
# total performance



# pvp performance

pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_crit_ratio,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score,
         ratio_pve_Hours,pve_kd,pve_hit_accuracy,pve_crit_ratio,pve_dps,pve_average_damage,pve_average_score)

# pve performance


```

#PCA

```{r}
# for total
with(pvz_df_nona, scatterplotMatrix(~total_kd + total_hit_accuracy + total_dps + total_average_damage + total_average_score, regLine = FALSE, diagonal = list(method = "histogram"), smooth=list(smoother=loessLine, spread = FALSE))) 

```

```{r}
#Correlation matrix

cor2 = with(pvz_df_nona, cor(cbind(pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score), method = "pearson"))
print(cor1)

cor2b = with(pvz_df_nona, corr.test(cbind(pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score), method = "pearson", adjust = "bonferroni"))
print(cor1b)

```
The similarity matrix above uses Pearson product moment correlations and assesses to what extent the variables move in the same direction at proportional strength. The Pearson correlation coefficient ranges from -1 to +1 and ignores the differences in the metric of the variables because it standardizes the variance to 1 and the mean to 0 for all variables, which is useful for comparing data measured on different scales. When squared the Pearson’s correlations are a PRE interpretation of the association. As can be seen from the correlation matrix above most variables in our model seem to have moderate to strong correlations with each other so we will proceed with the PCA.

To correct for multiple comparisons the Bonferroni correction is used. It reduces the risk that, with a large number of variables, significance is due to chance. It is performed by dividing a/n, where a is the significance level for the family of n tests. The Bonferroni correction is displayed in the last table in the output above and consists of the p-values above the diagonal. Notably, the vast majority of the p-values remain at 0.00 in both the corrected and the uncorrected versions. The one unusual p-value is that of pvp_kd with pvp_crit_ratio, where we find no statistically significant correlation after the Bonferroni correction, where there used to be one in the uncorrected version. 

```{r}
VSS.scree(cor2)
```
The scree plot plots the eigen values of the principal components on the y-axis sorted from highest to lowest, and the component number on the x-axis. This plot is used to evaluate how many principal components to retain for the PCA. The above scree plot is run on the correlation matrix using regular PCA eigenvalues with no rotation. As can be seen in the plot, the first component has a very large eigen value of around 4.4, the second has an eigen value around 1.2, then the third variable has an eigen value slightly above 1. The cut-off point of 1 is the baseline as 1 is effectively the average, and we only want to include factors which add something in terms of information. Hence, we want to include all the components above 1. Thus, we should include 3 components in our PCA, still it has to be noted that sometimes the scree plot tends to retain too few components, that’s why we also look at Kaisers rule and theoretical justification.

```{r}
pca2 = principal(cor2, nfactors = 5, rotate = "none")
loadings(pca2)

```
As can be seen above a naïve PCA is performed to examine the eigen values in the “ss loading” line of the output. Following Kaiser’s rule only principal components with eigen values over 1 should be included in a PCA. 
In this case those are the first two principal components. Together PC1, PC2,account for 76.2% of the variance in the dataset of the original 9 variables. So, we can reduce the dimensionality to two dimensions and still account for 76.2% of the variance, which appears to be a good balance between parsimony and retaining information.

#rotation
Rotating the factor loadings makes them more distinctive and understandable. The method used for the rotation is ‘Varimax’, a varimax rotation spins the axis whilst they remain perpendicular, so the factors are uncorrelated. This has the effect of differentiating the original variables as much as possible. It minimizes the variables that have high loadings on any one given factor, so each one has either small or large loadings on particular variables. It tends to yield results that make it as easy as possible to identify each variable with a single factor. Below a varimax rotation will be conducted.
```{r}
rc2 = principal(with(pvz_df_nona, cbind(total_kd,total_hit_accuracy,total_dps,total_average_damage,total_average_score)), nfactors = 1, rotate = "varimax")
loadings(rc2)

plot(rc2)

```
# create new variable
```{r}
pvz_df_nona$total_performance = rc2$scores[,1]

oii.summary(pvz_df_nona$total_performance, extended=TRUE)
```



```{r}
# for pvp
with(pvz_df_nona, scatterplotMatrix(~pvp_kd + pvp_hit_accuracy + pvp_dps + pvp_average_damage + pvp_average_score, regLine = FALSE, diagonal = list(method = "histogram"), smooth=list(smoother=loessLine, spread = FALSE))) 

```
```{r}
#Correlation matrix

cor1 = with(pvz_df_nona, cor(cbind(pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score), method = "pearson"))
print(cor1)

cor1b = with(pvz_df_nona, corr.test(cbind(pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score), method = "pearson", adjust = "bonferroni"))
print(cor1b)

```
The similarity matrix above uses Pearson product moment correlations and assesses to what extent the variables move in the same direction at proportional strength. The Pearson correlation coefficient ranges from -1 to +1 and ignores the differences in the metric of the variables because it standardizes the variance to 1 and the mean to 0 for all variables, which is useful for comparing data measured on different scales. When squared the Pearson’s correlations are a PRE interpretation of the association. As can be seen from the correlation matrix above most variables in our model seem to have moderate to strong correlations with each other so we will proceed with the PCA.

To correct for multiple comparisons the Bonferroni correction is used. It reduces the risk that, with a large number of variables, significance is due to chance. It is performed by dividing a/n, where a is the significance level for the family of n tests. The Bonferroni correction is displayed in the last table in the output above and consists of the p-values above the diagonal. Notably, the vast majority of the p-values remain at 0.00 in both the corrected and the uncorrected versions. The one unusual p-value is that of pvp_kd with pvp_crit_ratio, where we find no statistically significant correlation after the Bonferroni correction, where there used to be one in the uncorrected version. 

```{r}
VSS.scree(cor1)
```
The scree plot plots the eigen values of the principal components on the y-axis sorted from highest to lowest, and the component number on the x-axis. This plot is used to evaluate how many principal components to retain for the PCA. The above scree plot is run on the correlation matrix using regular PCA eigenvalues with no rotation. As can be seen in the plot, the first component has a very large eigen value of around 4.4, the second has an eigen value around 1.2, then the third variable has an eigen value slightly above 1. The cut-off point of 1 is the baseline as 1 is effectively the average, and we only want to include factors which add something in terms of information. Hence, we want to include all the components above 1. Thus, we should include 3 components in our PCA, still it has to be noted that sometimes the scree plot tends to retain too few components, that’s why we also look at Kaisers rule and theoretical justification.

```{r}
pca1 = principal(cor1, nfactors = 5, rotate = "none")
loadings(pca1)

```
As can be seen above a naïve PCA is performed to examine the eigen values in the “ss loading” line of the output. Following Kaiser’s rule only principal components with eigen values over 1 should be included in a PCA. 
In this case those are the first two principal components. Together PC1, PC2,account for 76.2% of the variance in the dataset of the original 9 variables. So, we can reduce the dimensionality to two dimensions and still account for 76.2% of the variance, which appears to be a good balance between parsimony and retaining information.

#rotation
Rotating the factor loadings makes them more distinctive and understandable. The method used for the rotation is ‘Varimax’, a varimax rotation spins the axis whilst they remain perpendicular, so the factors are uncorrelated. This has the effect of differentiating the original variables as much as possible. It minimizes the variables that have high loadings on any one given factor, so each one has either small or large loadings on particular variables. It tends to yield results that make it as easy as possible to identify each variable with a single factor. Below a varimax rotation will be conducted.
```{r}
rc1 = principal(with(pvz_df_nona, cbind(pvp_kd,pvp_hit_accuracy,pvp_dps,pvp_average_damage,pvp_average_score)), nfactors = 1, rotate = "varimax")
loadings(rc1)

plot(rc1)

```
# create new variable
```{r}
pvz_df_nona$pvp_performance = rc1$scores[,1]

oii.summary(pvz_df_nona$pvp_performance, extended=TRUE)
```

```{r}

```














```{r}
model_test <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(autonomy ~ Hours + total_kd))

summary(model_test)

#good
#Hours
# total_kd
# ratio_pvp_Hours    (CI is negative -> so the more you play PVP the worse wellbeing)
# ratio_pve_Hours  (CI is positive -> so the more you play PVE the better wellbeing)


#bad
#Pve, pvp Hours
#pve, pvp _kd
#total_average_score
# total dps total sps
```


```{r}
model_all <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + total_kd + total_dps +total_average_score + total_sps))

summary(model_all)
```
```{r}
with(pvz_df_nona, scatterplotMatrix( ~ Hours + total_kd + total_dps +total_average_score + total_sps, diagonal=list(method ="histogram", breaks="FD"), regLine = FALSE, smooth=list(spread=FALSE)))
```

```{r}
library(mctest)
eigprop(model_all, na.rm = TRUE, Inter = FALSE)
```


## linear regression with dummy variables for interaction effect


```{r}

# create variable
pvz_df_nona$player_type <- 0

pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="1"] <- "Casual Players"
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="2"] <- "PVP Noobs"
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="3"] <- "PVE Adventurers" 
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="4"] <- "Veterans" 
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="5"] <- "Elites" 

pvz_df_nona$player_type <- factor(pvz_df_nona$player_type, levels=c("Casual Players", "PVP Noobs", "PVE Adventurers" ,"Veterans"  ,"Elites"), labels=c("Casual Players", "PVP Noobs", "PVE Adventurers" ,"Veterans"  ,"Elites"))

# create dummy variables for player_type (clusters)

# cluster 1 == Elite : 

# cluster 2 == PVE adventurers: 

# cluster 3 ==  PVP Noobs:

# cluster 4 ==  ex-PVP Elite: 

# cluster 5 == Casual players: 

pvz_df_nona$c_elite <- 0
pvz_df_nona$c_elite[pvz_df_nona$cluster==5] <- 1 

pvz_df_nona$c_pve_adventurers <- 0
pvz_df_nona$c_pve_adventurers[pvz_df_nona$cluster==3] <- 1 

pvz_df_nona$c_pvp_noobs <- 0
pvz_df_nona$c_pvp_noobs[pvz_df_nona$cluster==2] <- 1 

pvz_df_nona$c_ex_pvp_elite <- 0
pvz_df_nona$c_ex_pvp_elite[pvz_df_nona$cluster==4] <- 1 

pvz_df_nona$c_casual_players <- 0
pvz_df_nona$c_casual_players[pvz_df_nona$cluster==1] <- 1 

```
