# set custon colour scheme
```{r}
#Create a custom color scale
library(RColorBrewer)
myColors <- brewer.pal(5,"Set2")
names(myColors) <- levels(pvz_df_nona$player_type)
colScale <- scale_colour_manual(name = "Player profile",values = myColors)
```

```{r}
myColors
```



1. regression with Hours only
```{r}
wellbeing_model_0 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours))
wellbeing_model_00 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + player_type))
wellbeing_model_01 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender))
wellbeing_model_011 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + c_elite))
wellbeing_model_012 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender * c_elite))# momdel 4
wellbeing_model_02 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + age))
wellbeing_model_03 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + age + player_type)) # momdel 4
wellbeing_model_04 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + age + player_type + Hours:player_type)) # model 5
wellbeing_model_05 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + player_type + Hours:player_type))
wellbeing_model_04 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + age + player_type + Hours:player_type)) # model 5
wellbeing_model_05 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + player_type + Hours:player_type))


tab_model(wellbeing_model_0,wellbeing_model_01, wellbeing_model_02)
oii.summary(wellbeing_model_01)

anova(wellbeing_model_0,wellbeing_model_01)
anova(wellbeing_model_0,wellbeing_model_00)
anova(wellbeing_model_01,wellbeing_model_02)
anova(wellbeing_model_01,wellbeing_model_011)
anova(wellbeing_model_011,wellbeing_model_012)
# adjusted r-squared of 0.01138, means that 1.1% of variance in wellbeing is explained by gametime

# “We	 constructed	a	linear	model	of	 wellbeing 	as	a	 function	 of	 gametime.	This	model	was	significant	(F(1,375)=5.329,	p<0.05).

# for every hour of gametime wellbeing increases by 0.015999.

# plot with one regression line to summarize the model
```
The R2 has a PRE interpretation and determines how good the model fit is. R2 is the percentage of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the independent variables. Low R2 (close to 0) implies a lot of variance in the dependent variable remains unexplained, high R2 (close to 1) means that the residual variance is small and that the independent variables are a good predictor of the dependent variable. The adjusted R2 is used instead of the multiple R2 because it takes into account the number of predictors in the model.

The adjusted R2 of ---- tells us that our independent variables explain ----% of the variance in DV and the p-value associated with our F- statistic (----P-value) confirms that the overall model is significant at any common level of significance. 

1 casual
2 pvp noobs
3 PVE
4 Veterans
5 ELitre
#visualize
```{r}
# plot of null model wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Game time (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") + 
     colScale +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12)) +
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-4, 7), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 120)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')




```

#centering
can be done to make the intercept more meaningfull
```{r}
pvz_df_nona$Hours.c = pvz_df_nona$Hours - mean(pvz_df_nona$Hours)

wellbeing_model_01 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours.c))
tab_model(wellbeing_model_01)
oii.summary(wellbeing_model_01)
```

# visualize centering
```{r}

# plot of null model wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Game time (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") + 
     colScale +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12)) +
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-4, 7), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')
```

```{r}
wellbeing_model_test <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_elite + c_pve_adventurers + c_pvp_noobs + c_ex_pvp_elite + c_casual_players  ))
summary(wellbeing_model_test)
```

```{r}
with(pvz_df_nona, scatterplotMatrix(~c_elite + c_pve_adventurers + c_pvp_noobs + c_ex_pvp_elite + c_casual_players, regLine = FALSE, diagonal = list(method = "histogram"), smooth=list(smoother=loessLine, spread = FALSE))) 
```

```{r}
wellbeing_model_test <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ 0+ Hours + player_type + gender + age ))
summary(wellbeing_model_test)
tab_model(wellbeing_model_test)
```

```{r}
wellbeing_model_coef <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender))
```

```{r}

mp3 <- coefplot(wellbeing_model_05,coefficients = c("player_typeElites","player_typeVeterans","player_typePVE Adventurers","player_typePVP Noobs"),
        outerCI = 0,
          innerCI = 1,
          title = "Intercept differences",
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
          color = c("#66C2A5"),
                   newNames = c("player_typeElites" ="Elites",
                       "player_typePVE Adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "player_typePVP Noobs"="PVP Noobs",
                       "player_typeVeterans"="Veterans",
                       "Hours:player_typeElites"="Game time : Elites",
                       "Hours:player_typePVE Adventurers"="Game time : PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:player_typePVP Noobs"="Game time : PVP Noobs",
                       "Hours:player_typeVeterans"="Game time : Veterans")
         )

mp4 <- coefplot(wellbeing_model_05,coefficients = c("Hours:player_typeElites","Hours:player_typeVeterans","Hours:player_typePVE Adventurers","Hours:player_typePVP Noobs"),
        outerCI = 0,
          innerCI = 1,
          title = "Slope differences",
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
         color = c("#FC8D62"),
                   newNames = c("player_typeElites" ="Elites",
                       "player_typePVE Adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "player_typePVP Noobs"="PVP Noobs",
                       "player_typeVeterans"="Veterans",
                       "Hours:player_typeElites"="Game time : Elites",
                       "Hours:player_typePVE Adventurers"="Game time : PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:player_typePVP Noobs"="Game time : PVP Noobs",
                       "Hours:player_typeVeterans"="Game time : Veterans")
         )
grid.arrange(mp3, mp4, nrow = 1)

summary(wellbeing_model_05)
```

```{r}

coefplot(wellbeing_model_04,coefficients = c("player_typeElites","player_typeVeterans","player_typePVE Adventurers","player_typePVP Noobs","Hours:player_typeElites","Hours:player_typeVeterans","Hours:player_typePVE Adventurers","Hours:player_typePVP Noobs"),
                outerCI = 0,
          innerCI = 1,
          title = "Coefficient plot",
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
          color = c("#66C2A5"),
         cex.var=1,
         col.pts=1,
                   newNames = c("player_typeElites" ="Elites",
                       "player_typePVE Adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "player_typePVP Noobs"="PVP Noobs",
                       "player_typeVeterans"="Veterans",
                       "Hours:player_typeElites"="Game time : Elites",
                       "Hours:player_typePVE Adventurers"="Game time : PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:player_typePVP Noobs"="Game time : PVP Noobs",
                       "Hours:player_typeVeterans"="Game time : Veterans"))



```

2. regression with Hours, Dummies and INTERACTION
```{r}


#intercept differences  (multiple linea regressions)
wellbeing_model_1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~gender  + Hours + c_elite ))
wellbeing_model_2 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours + c_pve_adventurers))
wellbeing_model_3 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours + c_pvp_noobs))
wellbeing_model_4 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours + c_ex_pvp_elite))
wellbeing_model_5 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours + c_casual_players))

#slope differences
wellbeing_model_6 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours * c_elite ))
wellbeing_model_7 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours * c_pve_adventurers))
wellbeing_model_8 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours * c_pvp_noobs))
wellbeing_model_9 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours * c_ex_pvp_elite))
wellbeing_model_10 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ gender  + Hours * c_casual_players))

tab_model(wellbeing_model_0, wellbeing_model_1, wellbeing_model_2, wellbeing_model_3,wellbeing_model_4,wellbeing_model_5,wellbeing_model_6, wellbeing_model_7, wellbeing_model_8, wellbeing_model_9,wellbeing_model_10, show.ci = FALSE)
```


res.aov3 <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_pve_adventurers, data = pvz_df_nona)
```{r}
# this one is significant (without age)
#intercept differences  (multiple linea regressions)
wellbeing_model_1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_elite ))
wellbeing_model_2 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_pve_adventurers))
wellbeing_model_3 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_pvp_noobs))
wellbeing_model_4 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_ex_pvp_elite))
wellbeing_model_5 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_casual_players))

#slope differences
wellbeing_model_6 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_elite ))
wellbeing_model_7 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_pve_adventurers))
wellbeing_model_8 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_pvp_noobs))
wellbeing_model_9 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_ex_pvp_elite))
wellbeing_model_10 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_casual_players))

tab_model(wellbeing_model_0, wellbeing_model_1, wellbeing_model_2, wellbeing_model_3,wellbeing_model_4,wellbeing_model_6, wellbeing_model_7, wellbeing_model_8, wellbeing_model_9, show.ci = FALSE)
```

```{r}
summary(wellbeing_model_05)
```


```{r}
wellbeing_model_elite <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender ))
wellbeing_model_elite1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + c_elite ))

anova(wellbeing_model_elite,wellbeing_model_elite1)
```


```{r}
#library(coefplot)

mp1 <- multiplot( wellbeing_model_9, wellbeing_model_8, wellbeing_model_7,wellbeing_model_6,wellbeing_model_0,
          coefficients = c("c_elite", "c_pve_adventurers","c_pvp_noobs","c_ex_pvp_elite"),
          outerCI = 0,
          innerCI = 1,
          title = "Intercept",
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
          decreasing = FALSE,
          legend.position = "none",
          newNames = c("c_elite" ="Elite",
                       "c_pve_adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "c_pvp_noobs"="PVP Noobs",
                       "c_ex_pvp_elite"="Veterans",
                       "c_casual_players"="Casual Players",
                       "Hours:c_elite"="Hours:Elites",
                       "Hours:c_pve_adventurers"="Hours:PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:c_pvp_noobs"="Hours:PVP Noobs",
                       "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite"="Hours:Veterans",
                       "Hours:c_casual_players"="Hours:Casual Players")
          
)


mp2 <- multiplot( wellbeing_model_9, wellbeing_model_8, wellbeing_model_7,wellbeing_model_6,wellbeing_model_0,
          coefficients = c("Hours:c_elite", "Hours:c_pve_adventurers", "Hours:c_pvp_noobs", "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite"),
          outerCI = 0,
          innerCI = 1,
          title = "Slope",
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
          decreasing = FALSE,
          legend.position = "none",
          newNames = c("c_elite" ="Elites",
                       "c_pve_adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "c_pvp_noobs"="PVP Noobs",
                       "c_ex_pvp_elite"="Veterans",
                       "c_casual_players"="Casual Players",
                       "Hours:c_elite"="Game time : Elites",
                       "Hours:c_pve_adventurers"="Game time : PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:c_pvp_noobs"="Game time : PVP Noobs",
                       "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite"="Game time : Veterans",
                       "Hours:c_casual_players"="Game time : Casual Players")
          
)

grid.arrange(mp1, mp2, nrow = 1)
```

```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df_nona$Hours)
hist(pvz_df_nona$Hours, breaks =120)
```
```{r}
oii.summary(pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance)
hist(pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance, breaks =120)
```

```{r}
mp2
```

```{r}
wellbeing_model_elite <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_elite ))
wellbeing_model_elite1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_elite ))

anova(wellbeing_model_elite,wellbeing_model_elite1)
```
```{r}
#significant
wellbeing_model_casual <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender ))
wellbeing_model_casual1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + c_casual_players ))

anova(wellbeing_model_casual,wellbeing_model_casual1)
```
```{r}
wellbeing_model_pvp_noobs <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender ))
wellbeing_model_pvp_noobs1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + c_pvp_noobs ))

anova(wellbeing_model_pvp_noobs,wellbeing_model_pvp_noobs1)
```

```{r}
# significant
wellbeing_model_pve_adventurers <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender ))
wellbeing_model_pve_adventurers1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + c_pve_adventurers ))

anova(wellbeing_model_pve_adventurers,wellbeing_model_pve_adventurers1)
```

```{r}
wellbeing_model_ex_pvp_elite <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender ))
wellbeing_model_ex_pvp_elite1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + c_ex_pvp_elite ))

anova(wellbeing_model_ex_pvp_elite,wellbeing_model_ex_pvp_elite1)
```

```{r}
#library(coefplot)

multiplot(wellbeing_model_10, wellbeing_model_9, wellbeing_model_8, wellbeing_model_7,wellbeing_model_6,wellbeing_model_0,
          coefficients = c("c_elite", "c_pve_adventurers","c_pvp_noobs","c_ex_pvp_elite", "c_casual_players", "Hours:c_elite", "Hours:c_pve_adventurers", "Hours:c_pvp_noobs", "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite", "Hours:c_casual_players", "Hours"),
          outerCI = 1,
          innerCI = 0,
          title = NULL,
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
          decreasing = FALSE,
          legend.position = "none",
          newNames = c("c_elite" ="Elite",
                       "c_pve_adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "c_pvp_noobs"="PVP noobs",
                       "c_ex_pvp_elite"="Veteran",
                       "c_casual_players"="Casual players",
                       "Hours:c_elite"="Hours:Elite",
                       "Hours:c_pve_adventurers"="Hours:PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:c_pvp_noobs"="Hours:PVP noobs",
                       "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite"="Hours:Veteran",
                       "Hours:c_casual_players"="Hours:Casual players")
          
)


```


```{r}
# Simple slope analysis with conditional intercepts
wellbeing_model_ss <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + Hours:player_type))
wellbeing_model_05 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + player_type + Hours:player_type))

wellbeing_ss <- sim_slopes(wellbeing_model_05,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx =player_type,
                  cond.int = TRUE,
                  jnplot = TRUE)

wellbeing_ss

```



```{r}

pvz_df_nona
```

# visualize
```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Game time (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     colScale +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12)) +
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-4, 7), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F)   +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')  


interact_plot(wellbeing_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=TRUE,
                  plot.points = TRUE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Wellbeing",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)

interact_plot(wellbeing_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Wellbeing",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)


```





# visualize
```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Hours") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-5, 6.0), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 120)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F)   +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')  
```

```{r}
coef(wellbeing_model_10)
coef(wellbeing_model_9)
coef(m2_wellbeing)
```

# visualize
```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, colour =player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     colScale +
     xlab("Gametime (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12))+ 
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-3, 6.0), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 65)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[1,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[1,1], color= myColors[1]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[2,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[2,1], color= myColors[2]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[3,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[3,1], color= myColors[3]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[4,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[4,1], color= myColors[4]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[5,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[5,1], color= myColors[5]) +
     geom_abline(slope=fixef(m1_wellbeing)[2], intercept = fixef(m1_wellbeing)[1], color= 'black',linetype =2) 
```

4. Anova
If F-test for change in R-squared is significant for the third regression, that means there is interaction effect


```{r}
ModelX <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_elite + c_pve_adventurers + c_pvp_noobs + c_ex_pvp_elite, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(ModelX)

ModelXM <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_elite + c_pve_adventurers + c_pvp_noobs + c_ex_pvp_elite+ Hours:c_elite + Hours:c_pve_adventurers + Hours:c_pvp_noobs + Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite, data = pvz_df_nona)

summary(ModelXM)

anova(ModelX,ModelXM)
```


```{r}
res.aov2 <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_pve_adventurers, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(res.aov2)

res.aov3 <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_pve_adventurers, data = pvz_df_nona)

summary(res.aov3)

anova(res.aov2,res.aov3)
```
```{r}
elite1 <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_elite, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(elite1)

elite2 <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * c_elite, data = pvz_df_nona)

summary(elite2)

anova(elite1,elite2)

```

From the ANOVA results, you can conclude the following, based on the p-values and a significance level of 0.05:

the p-value of Hours is 0.0207 (significant), which indicates that gametime is associated with wellbeing.
the p-value of c_pve_adventurers is 0.0142 (significant), which indicates that being a PVE Adventurer is postively associated with subjective wellbeing
the p-value for the interaction between Hours:c_pve_adventurers is 0.7250  (insignificant), which indicates that the relationships between gametime and wellbeing doe not depend on pve adventurer.




```{r}
res <- lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + player_type + gender + Hours:player_type, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(res)
cbind(coef(res), confint(res, level = 0.95))

library(car)

res.std <- rstandard(res)
plot(res.std, ylab="Standardized Residuals")

"Outlier Test. Observations with a Bonferroni p < .01 might be considered as outliers and might need further investigation."
outlierTest(res)

"Residual plots, curvature tests and normality plot"
residualPlots(res)
ggplot(as.data.frame(res.std), aes(sample = res.std)) +
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line()

"Variance inflation factor (VIF >=5 indicates high level of multicollinearity)"
vif(res)

"Estimated Marginal Means"
library(emmeans)

m_Hours<- mean(pvz_df_nona$Hours, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_Hours<- sd(pvz_df_nona$Hours, na.rm = TRUE)


emm <- emmeans(res,  ~ Hours*player_type,
  cov.keep = 3, at = list(
  Hours = c(m_Hours-sd_Hours, m_Hours, m_Hours+sd_Hours)), level = 0.95)
summary(emm)

simpleSlope <- emtrends(res, pairwise ~ player_type, var = "Hours", level = 0.95)
summary(simpleSlope)

emmip(res, player_type ~ Hours,
  cov.keep = 3, at = list(
  Hours = c(m_Hours-sd_Hours, m_Hours, m_Hours+sd_Hours)),
  CIs = TRUE, level = 0.95, position = "jitter")


```



Tukey multiple pairwise comparisons
```{r}
res.aov4 <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * player_type, data = pvz_df_nona)

TukeyHSD(res.aov4, which = "player_type")

```







2. Regression with Hours and the Dummies
```{r}
wellbeing_model_test <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + c_elite + c_pve_adventurers + c_pvp_noobs + c_ex_pvp_elite))

anova(wellbeing_model_test,wellbeing_model_0)

```


```{r}
# choose ommited category

boxplot(spane_game_balance ~ player_type, data = pvz_df_nona)
```
The omitted dummy category for labour force is student, because theoretically it is the most different as student generally speaking have not entered the labor force yet; this is supported by the grouped boxplot which demonstrates that students is indeed the most different category. Students is clearly associated with higher/lower levels of trust than employed, retired, unemployed. The grouped boxplot also highlights some potential outliers in the students and students categories. These will be noted and discussed later on in the analysis.



3. Regression with the Dummies, and the Interactions
```{r}

```

```{r}

```
4. Anova
If F-test for change in R-squared is significant for the third regression, that means there is interaction effect
```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

```{r}

```

https://m-clark.github.io/mixed-models-with-R/random_intercepts.html


## normal fixed effect model


1. regression with Hours only
```{r}
wellbeing_model_0 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours))
tab_model(wellbeing_model_0)
oii.summary(wellbeing_model_0)

# adjusted r-squared of 0.01138, means that 1.1% of variance in wellbeing is explained by gametime

# “We	 constructed	a	linear	model	of	 wellbeing 	as	a	 function	 of	 gametime.	This	model	was	significant	(F(1,375)=5.329,	p<0.05).

# for every hour of gametime wellbeing increases by 0.015999.

# plot with one regression line to summarize the model
```
The R2 has a PRE interpretation and determines how good the model fit is. R2 is the percentage of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the independent variables. Low R2 (close to 0) implies a lot of variance in the dependent variable remains unexplained, high R2 (close to 1) means that the residual variance is small and that the independent variables are a good predictor of the dependent variable. The adjusted R2 is used instead of the multiple R2 because it takes into account the number of predictors in the model.

The adjusted R2 of ---- tells us that our independent variables explain ----% of the variance in DV and the p-value associated with our F- statistic (----P-value) confirms that the overall model is significant at any common level of significance. 


This would be fine except that we are ignoring the clustering. A side effect of doing so is that our standard errors are biased, and thus claims about statistical significance based on them would be off. More importantly however is that we simply don’t get to explore the player profile effect as laid out by RQ2.

An alternative approach would be to run separate regressions for every student. However, there are many drawbacks to this- it’s not easily summarized when there are many groups, typically there would be very little data within each cluster to do so (as in this case), and the models are over-contextualized, meaning they ignore what students have in common. We’ll compare such an approach to the mixed model later.

# visualize
```{r}
# plot of null model wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     colScale +
     xlab("Gametime (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12))+ 
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-3, 6.0), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 65)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')
```


## Diagnostics

```{r}
# fit vs residuals
library(calibrate)
pvz_df_nona$fit1 = fitted.values(wellbeing_model_0)
pvz_df_nona$case = rep(1:nrow(pvz_df_nona))
pvz_df_nona$student1 = rstudent(wellbeing_model_0)
plot(pvz_df_nona$fit1, pvz_df_nona$student1)
abline(h = 0)
textxy(pvz_df_nona$fit1, pvz_df_nona$student1, pvz_df_nona$case)

```

```{r}
# influence plot for outliers
influencePlot(wellbeing_model_0)
```


```{r}
# heteroscedasticity
ncvTest(wellbeing_model_0)

```


```{r}
# normality
plot(wellbeing_model_0)
```




In line with different studies, we find a similar relation between gametime and wellbeing. However when plotting the identified clusters it becomes evident that thsi relationship might depend on different playstyles. To test this hypothesis Multilevel modelling will be used.

First a null model is run to determine the suitability of multilevel modeling. After which a MLM is run to see if the clusters 


## 2.  Multilevel model 

# packages
```{r}
library(lme4)
library(sjPlot)
library(lmerTest)
```
# colours
```{r}
cols = brewer.pal(5, "Set2")
```

# MEAN centering the data
```{r}

cols
```

# 2.1. Unconditional model 
In the unconditional model, only the dependent variable and the grouping variable(s) (e.g., subject ID) are entered. No predictors are entered, thus the model is not “conditioned” upon any predictor variables.
```{r}
m0_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ 1 + ( 1 | player_type), data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(m0_wellbeing)
tab_model(m0_wellbeing)
```

# Output & interpretation
```{r}
p_value(m0_wellbeing,p.kr=TRUE)
```
Test suitability fo using a LML

 The most relevant for evaluating an unconditional model is the computation of p-values based on conditional F-tests with Kenward-Roger approximation for the degrees of freedom. If it is < .05 (or whatever predetermined cutoff for significance is being used), the model can be interpreted as showing significant between-cluster variation and thus supporting the use of multilevel modeling. (ref LML for R book)


ICC 



# 2.2. random intercept effect model - Interpretation and explanation of the intra-class correlation.
```{r}
m1_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + age + (1 | player_type), data = pvz_df_nona, REML =FALSE)
```

```{r}
coef(m1_wellbeing)
```

# set custon colour scheme
```{r}
#Create a custom color scale
library(RColorBrewer)
myColors <- brewer.pal(5,"Set2")
names(myColors) <- levels(pvz_df_nona$player_type)
colScale <- scale_colour_manual(name = "Player profile",values = myColors)
```

```{r}
myColors
```

# visualize
```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, colour =player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     colScale +
     xlab("Gametime (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12))+ 
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-3, 6.0), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 65)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[1,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[1,1], color= myColors[1]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[2,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[2,1], color= myColors[2]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[3,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[3,1], color= myColors[3]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[4,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[4,1], color= myColors[4]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[5,2], intercept = coef(m1_wellbeing)$player_type[5,1], color= myColors[5]) +
     geom_abline(slope=fixef(m1_wellbeing)[2], intercept = fixef(m1_wellbeing)[1], color= 'black',linetype =2) 
```

# Output & interpretation
```{r}
summary(m1_wellbeing)
ranef(m1_wellbeing)
coef(m1_wellbeing)
tab_model(m1_wellbeing)
```
The (1|player_type) means that we are allowing the intercept, represented by 1, to vary by player profile. With the mixed model, we get the same results as the regression, but as we’ll see we’ll have more to talk about.


The “fixed parts” section of this output can be interpreted in a similar manner to OLS regression. The information in the “random parts” section helps researchers partition the variance in the dependent variable. Partitioning the variance refers to identifying how much variance in the dependent variable is due to within-person (σ2) and between-person (τ00) variance.

# compute intra-class correlation (ICC) as pseudo R-squared  

Another way to interpret the variance output is to note percentage of the student variance out of the total, or 0.064 / 0.122 = 52%. This is also called the intraclass correlation, because it is also an estimate of the within cluster correlation, as we’ll see later.

 For example, in Figure 5, the ICC is .436, which is calculated by dividing between-person variance and the sum of between- and within-person variance (i.e., ICC = τ00/( σ2 + τ00), or 0.436 = 2.695/(2.695+3.429)). The ICC in this example would be interpreted as meaning that 46.4% of the variance in suicidal ideation scores are due to person-to-person variation, whereas 53.6% (i.e., 1-0.464) of the variance is due to within-person observation-to-observation variation. As noted above, there are no strong guidelines for interpreting an ICC, however scores approaching 1.0 would indicate that nearly all variation is occurring at the highest level (in this case, person-level) and could mean that multilevel
# anova
```{r}

```



# 3. interaction effect model
```{r}
m2_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + gender + age+ ( Hours | player_type), data = pvz_df_nona)
```

# visualize
```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Hours") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-5, 6.0), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 120)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F)   +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')  
```

# visualize
```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, colour =player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     colScale +
     xlab("Gametime (Hours)") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     theme(text=element_text(family="Times New Roman", size=12))+ 
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-3, 6.0), breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 65)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[1,2], intercept = coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[1,1], color= myColors[1]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[2,2], intercept = coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[2,1], color= myColors[2]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[3,2], intercept = coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[3,1], color= myColors[3]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[4,2], intercept = coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[4,1], color= myColors[4]) +
     geom_abline(slope=coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[5,2], intercept = coef(m2_wellbeing)$player_type[5,1], color= myColors[5]) +
     geom_abline(slope=fixef(m2_wellbeing)[2], intercept = fixef(m2_wellbeing)[1], color= 'black',linetype =2) +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')  
```


# summarize 
```{r}
summary(m2_wellbeing)
tab_model(m2_wellbeing)
fixef(m2_wellbeing)
coef(m2_wellbeing)
ranef(m2_wellbeing)
```

The (fixed) effect of game time (Hours) is significant. This is the average effect of Hours taken over the whole population of players. 
We know that every cluster has its own random deviation of this effect. Hence, the relevant question is whether the cluster profiles just fluctuate around a constant Hours effect whether the Hours effect is substantially larger than this cluster specific hours variation.
Technically speaking, the playstyle (cluster) specific game time (Hours) variation is nothing else than the interaction between playstyle (cluster) and  game time (Hours).

There is no straightforward R2, instead ther is a marginal R2 and conditional R2. The difference is that the marginal R2 reflects the variance of the fixed effects only, whereas the conditional R2 reflects both the fixed and random effects. It is nice to report these, but for multilevel models it is also (more) common and good practice to evalute model fit by comparing different models (as discussed below).

and with iCC??



# anova
```{r}
#anova(m0_wellbeing,m1_wellbeing,m2_wellbeing)
tab_model(wellbeing_model_0,m0_wellbeing,m1_wellbeing,m2_wellbeing)


# lowest BICC AND AIC is M1
# but they are 
```
The (fixed) effect of game time (Hours) is significant.



The random intercept model is significasnt

the random slop and intercept model is significnt and performs the best (highest ICC and highest R2)
Still it does not explain a lot of the wellebing
But it explains it mroe and significant;y then just looking at hours!



4. assumptions
4.1 colineairty - residual vs fitted values plot
4.2 influential points  -influence plot
4.3 homoscedasity
4.4 normality  - qqplot
4.5 independence - reason we did MLM
# Tukey-Anscombe plot
```{r}
plot(m_ri_wellbeing)
# The Tukey-Anscombe plot looks good
```
# QQ- plots
```{r}
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
qqnorm(ranef(m_ri_wellbeing)$player_type[, 1], main = "Random effects of Playstyle")
qqnorm(resid(m_ri_wellbeing), main = "Residuals")
```

## random effects

```{r}
ranef(m2_wellbeing)

coef(m2_wellbeing)
coef(wellbeing_model_0)
  
```

## Interpretation of final model
```{r}
plot_model(m2_wellbeing, type='re')

# colour indicates if it is positive or negative

```
The fixed effects are the coefficients (intercept, slope) as we usually think about them. The random effects are the variances of the intercepts
or slopes across groups.

Because we may be interested in the variation of the coefficients across groups as a research
question

and we are. so whilst the relation between gametime and wellbeing is small and positive, this differs between players profiles. As can be seen from the random effects plot, there are slight differences per profile. For some this relationship between gametime and wellbeing is more positive such

In fixed-effects models (e.g., regression, ANOVA, generalized linear models), there is only one source of random variability.
We call the variability across individuals’ “residual” variance (in linear models, this is the estimate of σ2, also called the mean squared error). It’s the variability that was unexplained by the predictors in the model (the fixed effects).

Mixed effects models are different in that there is more than one source of random variability in the data.

In addition to individual players, there may also be random variability across the clusters of players.

We account for these differences through the incorporation of random effects. Random intercepts allow the outcome to be higher or lower for each cluster; random slopes allow fixed effects to vary for each cluster.

In other words, we can now incorporate (instead of ignore) playstyle-to-playstyle variability in player wellbeing, and improve our ability to describe how playtime (fixed effects) relate to wellbeing (outcomes).

We can also talk directly about the variability of random effects, similar to how we talk about residual variance in linear models.

There is no general measure of whether variability is large or small, but subject-matter experts can consider standard deviations of random effects relative to the outcomes.

For example, if playstyle-averaged wellebeing only varies from the overall average with an SD of 0.02 , the clusters may be considered rather uniform; however, if cluster-averaged wellbeing varied from the overall average with an SD of 0.5, it would seem as if playstyle makes a large difference in player wellbeing.

# Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)

Observations from the same cluster are usually more similar to each other than observations from different clusters.  If they are, you can’t use statistical methods on these data to that assume independence, because estimates of variance, and therefore p-values, will be incorrect.

Mixed models not only account for the correlations among observations in the same cluster, they give you an estimate of that correlation.

The ratio of the between-cluster variance to the total variance is called the Intraclass Correlation.  It tells you the proportion of the total variance in Y that is accounted for by the clustering.

WHy ICC?

1. It can help you determine whether or not a linear mixed model is even necessary. If you find that the correlation is zero, that means the observations within clusters are no more similar than observations from different clusters.  Go ahead and use a simpler analysis technique.

2. It can be theoretically meaningful to understand how much of the overall variation in the response is explained simply by clustering.  you can tell to what extent wellbeing varies among playstyles, but not among players or varies little on average among playstyles, but varies a lot across players.

```{r}
coefplot(m2_wellbeing)
```

```{r}
#library(coefplot)

multiplot(wellbeing_model_10, wellbeing_model_9, wellbeing_model_8, wellbeing_model_7,wellbeing_model_6,wellbeing_model_0,
          coefficients = c("c_elite", "c_pve_adventurers","c_pvp_noobs","c_ex_pvp_elite", "c_casual_players", "Hours:c_elite", "Hours:c_pve_adventurers", "Hours:c_pvp_noobs", "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite", "Hours:c_casual_players", "Hours"),
          outerCI = 0,
          title = NULL,
          xlab = "Regression coefficient",
          ylab = NULL,
          decreasing = FALSE,
          legend.position = "none",
          newNames = c("c_elite" ="Elite",
                       "c_pve_adventurers" ="PVE Adventurers",
                       "c_pvp_noobs"="PVP noobs",
                       "c_ex_pvp_elite"="Ex-PVP Elite",
                       "c_casual_players"="Casual players",
                       "Hours:c_elite"="Hours:Elite",
                       "Hours:c_pve_adventurers"="Hours:PVE Adventurers",
                       "Hours:c_pvp_noobs"="Hours:PVP noobs",
                       "Hours:c_ex_pvp_elite"="Hours:Ex-PVP Elite",
                       "Hours:c_casual_players"="Hours:Casual players")
          
)


```



### LLM form bookdown

m_ri_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + ( Hours | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)
```{r}

#Model A (Unconditional means model)
model.a <- lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + (1 | player_type), REML = T, data = pvz_df_nona)

summary(model.a)
```

```{r}
# 2.98 is the estimated mean wellbeing across all players
# 3.60 is the estimated variance in within player deviations
# 0.02 is the estimated variance in within cluster deviations

2.98/(3.60+0.02) = 0.823
```

The relative levels of between- and within-cluster variabilities can be compared through the intraclass correlation coefficient:
 
Thus, 82.3% of the total variability in wellbeing scores are attributable to differences among subjects. In this particular model, we can also say that the average correlation for any pair of responses from the same cluster is a high 0.823. As  ρ approaches 0, responses from an cluster are essentially independent and accounting for the multilevel structure of the data becomes less crucial. However, as  ρ approaches 1, observations from the same cluster essentially provide no additional information and accounting for the multilevel structure becomes very important. With ρ near 0, the effective sample size (the number of independent pieces of information we have for modeling) approaches the total number of observations, while with  ρ near 1, the effective sample size approaches the number of subjects in the study.

```{r}
#Random Slopes and Intercepts Model
model.b <- lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + (1+ Hours | player_type), REML = T, data = pvz_df_nona)

summary(model.b)
```

# 2.76 is the estimated mean wellbeing 
# 0.028 is the mean increase in wellbeing per hour gametime
# 3.52 is the  variance in within cluster deviations
# 0.03 is the  variance in between cluster deviations in wellebing per hour gametime
# 0.0001536 the variance in between-cluster deviations in increases in wellbeing per hour gametime

# 0.89 the correlation in subjects’ wellbeing per hour gametime and their differences in wellbeing per hour gametime 

Pseude R squared (model comparrison) (3.60 -3.52) / 3.60  = 0.02222222222

The estimated within-cluster variance decreased by 2.2% (from 3.6 to 3.5) from the unconditional means model, implying that only 2.2% of within-cluster variability in wellbeing scores can be explained by performance type. This calculation is considered a pseudo R-squared value:
















```{r}
plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$enjoyment, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$competence, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$autonomy, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$relatedness, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
```
As you can see there might be differences in player type between the relations

## null model
```{r}
nm_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ 1 +( 1 | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(nm_wellbeing)
tab_model(nm_wellbeing)

nm_enjoyment = lm(enjoyment ~ Hours, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(nm_enjoyment)

nm_competence = lm(competence ~ Hours, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(nm_competence)

nm_autonomy = lm(autonomy ~ Hours, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(nm_autonomy)  # only this one is not significant

nm_relatedness = lm(relatedness ~ Hours, data = pvz_df_nona)
summary(nm_relatedness)
```

```{r}

m_ri_wellbeing_ex = lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + competence + autonomy + relatedness, data = pvz_df_nona)

oii.summary(m_ri_wellbeing_ex)
```


# plot the null model
```{r}
# plot of null model wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Hours") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     ylim(-3, 6.5) +
     xlim(0, 75) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')
```
# random intercepts
```{r}
m_ri_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours +( 1 | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)

m_ri_wellbeing_ex = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + competence + autonomy + relatedness + (1 | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)

m_ri_autonomy = lmer(autonomy ~ Hours +(1 | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)
```

```{r}
coef (m_ri_wellbeing)
```

# plot random intercepts models
```{r}
plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
for (i in pvz_df_nona$cluster) {  ## for each cluster
  abline(coef(m_ri_wellbeing)$cluster[i,1], coef(m_ri_wellbeing)$cluster[i,2], col=cols[i])
}

plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$autonomy, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
for (i in pvz_df_nona$cluster) {  ## for each cluster
  abline(coef(m_ri_wellbeing)$cluster[i,1], coef(m_ri_wellbeing)$cluster[i,2], col=cols[i])
}
```

```{r}
tab_model(m_ri_wellbeing)
# There is still a positive effect of hours, and it is even mroe significant now that it takes into account the different groups of players
```

```{r}
tab_model(m_ri_wellbeing_ex)
```

```{r}
tab_model(m_ri_autonomy)
# autonomy is still insignificant
```


## random intercept and slope model 
```{r}
m_rs_wellbeing = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + ( 1 + Hours | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)

m_rs_wellbeing_ex = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + age + gender + ( 1 + Hours | cluster), data = pvz_df_nona)

```

```{r}
coef (m_rs_wellbeing)

fixef (m_rs_wellbeing)

ranef(m_rs_wellbeing)
```

```{r}
oii.summary(m_rs_wellbeing)
```






```{r}
plot_test1 <-plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
  for (i in pvz_df_nona$cluster) {  ## for each cluster
  abline(coef(m_ri_wellbeing)$cluster[i,1], coef(m_ri_wellbeing)$cluster[i,2], col=cols[i])
  }

```

```{r}
plot_test <- plot(pvz_df_nona$Hours, pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance, col=cols[pvz_df_nona$cluster], pch=16)
            for (i in pvz_df_nona$cluster) {  ## for each cluster
            abline(coef(m_rs_wellbeing)$cluster[i,1], coef(m_rs_wellbeing)$cluster[i,2], col=cols[i])
}
```

```{r}
# visualise wellbeing
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = enjoyment, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Hours") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 6.0), breaks = c(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6)) +
     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 75)) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F)   +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2, col = 'black')


# prefered not to say and other had not enough data
```
```{r}
# visualise enjoyment
ggplot(pvz_df_nona, aes(x = Hours, y = enjoyment, color = player_type, shape = player_type) ) +
     geom_point() +
     xlab("Hours") +
     ylab("Wellbeing") +
     ylim(0, 7.5) +
     xlim(0, 50) +
     labs(shape="Player profile",col="Player profile") +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', fullrange =TRUE, se = F)   +
     geom_smooth(method = 'lm', se = F, aes(group = 1),linetype =2) +
  facet_wrap('player_type')
```
```{r}
wellbeing_plot2 <- interact_plot(wellbeing_model,
                  pred= "Hours",
                  modx = "player_type",
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Wellbeing",
                  legend.main = "Player profile",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

wellbeing_plot2

# this shows that onle PvE adventurers start at a higher wellbeing than all the others, the summary shows that it is significantly different from Casual players
```

# comparing models 
```{r}
m_base = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ (1 | cluster), data=pvz_df_nona)
m1 = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + (1 | cluster), data=pvz_df_nona)
m2 = lmer(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + (1 + Hours| cluster), data=pvz_df_nona)

a1 = anova(m_base,m1,m2)
a1
```

```{r}
tab_model(m_base, m1, m2)
```


## anova

```{r}
one.way <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + player_type, data=pvz_df_nona)
interaction <- aov(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * player_type, data=pvz_df_nona)

summary(one.way)
summary(interaction)
```


```{r}
plot(one.way)
```
```{r}
tukey.one.way<-TukeyHSD(one.way, which = 'player_type')

tukey.one.way

tukey.interaction<-TukeyHSD(interaction, which = 'player_type')

tukey.interaction
```

```{r}

```










## interaction effects with dummies
```{r}
library(jtools)
```

```{r}

# create variable
pvz_df_nona$player_type <- 0

pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="1"] <- "Elite"
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="2"] <- "PVE Adventurers"
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="3"] <- "PVP Noobs" 
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="4"] <- "ex-PVP Elite" 
pvz_df_nona$player_type[pvz_df_nona$cluster=="5"] <- "Casual players" 
```

```{r}
oii.freq(pvz_df_nona$player_type)
```

```{r}
testmodel <- lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * player_type, data=pvz_df_nona)
interact_plot(testmodel, pred = "Hours", modx = "player_type", plot.points = TRUE)
```


```{r}
#NO
wellbeing_model <- lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * gender, data=pvz_df_nona)

wellbeing_ss <- sim_slopes(wellbeing_model,
                  pred= Hours,
                  modx = gender,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

wellbeing_ss


wellbeing_plot <- interact_plot(wellbeing_model,
                  pred= "Hours",
                  modx = "gender",
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Wellbeing",
                  legend.main = "Player profile",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

wellbeing_plot
```

```{r}
tab_model(wellbeing_model)
```

```{r}
#YES PVP Noobs, and Hours *Elite
enjoyment_model <- lm(enjoyment ~ Hours * player_type, data=pvz_df_nona)

enjoyment_ss <- sim_slopes(enjoyment_model,
                  pred= Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

enjoyment_ss


enjoyment_plot <- interact_plot(enjoyment_model,
                  pred= "Hours",
                  modx = "player_type",
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Enjoyment",
                  legend.main = "Player profile",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

enjoyment_plot
```

```{r}
tab_model(enjoyment_model)
```


```{r}
# 3. YES Hours*Elite
extrinsic_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(extrinsic ~ Hours * player_type))

extrinsic_ss <- sim_slopes(extrinsic_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

extrinsic_ss

extrinsic_plot <- interact_plot(extrinsic_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Extrinsic motivation",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

extrinsic_plot
```

```{r}
tab_model(extrinsic_model)
```

```{r}
#YEs PVP Noobs
competence_model <- lm(competence ~ Hours * player_type, data=pvz_df_nona)

competence_ss <- sim_slopes(competence_model,
                  pred= Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

competence_ss


competence_plot <- interact_plot(competence_model,
                  pred= "Hours",
                  modx = "player_type",
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Competence",
                  legend.main = "Player profile",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

competence_plot
```

```{r}
tab_model(competence_model)
```

```{r}
# 3. NO
autonomy_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(autonomy ~ Hours * player_type))

autonomy_ss <- sim_slopes(autonomy_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

autonomy_ss

autonomy_plot <- interact_plot(autonomy_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="Play time (Hours)",
                  y.label ="Autonomy",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

autonomy_plot
```
```{r}
tab_model(autonomy_model)
```

```{r}
# 3. yes, ex-PVP
relatedness_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(relatedness ~ Hours * player_type))

relatedness_ss <- sim_slopes(relatedness_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

relatedness_ss

relatedness_plot <- interact_plot(relatedness_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Relatedness",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE, 
                  plot.points = TRUE)

relatedness_plot
```

```{r}
tab_model(relatedness_model)
```

```{r}
tab_model(wellbeing_model, enjoyment_model, extrinsic_model, competence_model, autonomy_model,relatedness_model)
```

# make visualisation
```{r}
library(gridExtra)
library(ggpubr)

```

```{r}
figure3 <- ggarrange(wellbeing_plot, enjoyment_plot, extrinsic_plot, competence_plot, autonomy_plot, relatedness_plot,
                    ncol = 3, nrow = 2, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "right")
figure3
```




```{r}
library(lsmeans)
```

```{r}

anova(wellbeing_model)
 
# Obtain slopes
wellbeing_model$coefficients
m.lst <- lstrends(wellbeing_model, "player_type", var="Hours")


# Compare slopes
pairs(m.lst)

```

```{r}
library(psych)
library(data.table)
```

```{r}
pvz_df_nona2 <- as.data.table(pvz_df_nona)
# Calculate Pearson's R
m.correlations <- pvz_df_nona2[, cor(spane_game_balance, Hours), by = player_type]
m.correlations
# Compare R values with Fisher's R to Z
paired.r(m.correlations[player_type=="Elite", V1], m.correlations[player_type=="Casual players", V1], 
         n = pvz_df_nona2[player_type %in% c("Elite", "Casual players"), .N])
paired.r(m.correlations[player_type=="Elite", V1], m.correlations[player_type=="PVP Noobs", V1], 
         n = pvz_df_nona2[player_type %in% c("Elite", "PVP Noobs"), .N])
paired.r(m.correlations[player_type=="PVP Noobs", V1], m.correlations[player_type=="Casual players", V1], 
         n = pvz_df_nona2[player_type %in% c("PVP Noobs", "Casual playersr"), .N])
```


```{r}
wellbeing_model
```

```{r}
tab_model(wellbeing_model)
```


## 2. run regressions with all relevant variables



**next step**

create limited dataframe with relevant variables 

create latent variables for player skill and game performance 

do descriptives and visualise those (also the ones done above like distiubuition of gametypes)

look at the analysis done by mattie and copy it with the new variables

##  moderated mulitple linear regression
Three-step Differential prediction process (Cleary, 1968)
1. Estimate the criterion-related validity (Simple linear regression)
2. Intercept differences: additive multiple linear regression model
3. Slope differences: Multiple linear regression model (multiplicative model), this is where moderated linear regression comes in.


# See if there is a relationship between time spent and wellbeing in each game mode
```{r}
# pvp
# Grand-mean center continous predictor or moderator varaibles
pvz_df_nona$c_Hours <- pvz_df_nona$Hours - mean(pvz_df_nona$Hours, na.rm = TRUE)

pvz_df_nona$c_total_kd <- pvz_df_nona$total_kd - mean(pvz_df_nona$total_kd, na.rm = TRUE)

# there is spane_balance and spane_game_balance
pvz_df_nona$c_spane_balance <- pvz_df_nona$spane_balance - mean(pvz_df_nona$spane_balance, na.rm = TRUE)

pvz_df_nona$c_spane_game_balance <- pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance - mean(pvz_df_nona$spane_game_balance, na.rm = TRUE)

```

```{r}
# assume all regression assumptions where met
# do regression diagnostics

# wellbeing and hours and KDA -- GOOD
model1 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(c_spane_game_balance ~ c_Hours + c_total_kd))
summary(model1)
```

```{r}
install.packages("interactions")
library(interactions)
```

```{r}
kd_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(c_spane_game_balance ~ c_Hours * c_total_kd))

kd_ss <- sim_slopes(kd_model,
                  pred=c_Hours,
                  modx = c_total_kd,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

kd_ss

kd_plot <- interact_plot(kd_model,
                  pred=c_Hours,
                  modx = c_total_kd,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Wellbeing",
                  legend.main = "kd",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)

kd_plot
```

```{r}
model2 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + total_average_damage))
summary(model2)
```
```{r}
model3 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + total_average_score))
summary(model3)
```
```{r}
model4 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + total_hit_accuracy))
summary(model4)
```
```{r}
model5 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + total_crit_ratio))
summary(model5)
```
```{r}
model6 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + total_crit_ratio))
summary(model6)
```


# see if it is moderated with performance metrics
```{r}

```


# if so, see if in-game performance predicts competence as a way to explain the the moderating factor.
```{r}

model7 <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(competence ~ total_kd))
summary(model7)
```

```{r}
#pvz_df$Hours10 <- pvz_df$Hours / 10

model3 <- with(pvz_df, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours ))
summary(model3)
```

```{r}

# subset df for players who played any pvp or pve
pvp_pvz_df <- filter(pvz_df, pvp_Hours > 0)
pve_pvz_df <- filter(pvz_df, pve_Hours > 0)

# subset df for players who played pvp
pvp_cluster_pvz_df <- filter(pvz_df, cluster == 2)
pve_cluster_pvz_df <- filter(pvz_df, cluster == 1)

# create variable
pvz_df$player_type <- 0

pvz_df$player_type[pvz_df$cluster=="1"] <- "PvP"
pvz_df$player_type[pvz_df$cluster=="2"] <- "PvE" 
```


```{r}
model4 <- with(pvz_df, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours + competence + autonomy + relatedness))
summary(model4)
```

```{r}
model5 <- with(pvp_cluster_pvz_df, lm(spane_balance ~ pvp_Hours + pvp_kd + pvp_hit_accuracy + pvp_crit_ratio + pvp_average_score + pvp_average_damage))
summary(model5)
```



```{r}
install.packages("interactions")
library(interactions)
```
```{r}
library(interactions)
```


have to make dummies for each and make FEM interaction effect
you can maybe plot them in the same plot.
and make a table of all the regression coefficients

```{r}
# 3. Slope differences WELLBEING  -- YES there is a stronger positive associaiton between time played and wellebing for pvp than pve players. At low hours of play pvp players have worse wellbeing than pve players, however at high hours pvp players have a better wellbeing then pve players

wellbeing_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * player_type))

wellbeing_ss <- sim_slopes(wellbeing_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

wellbeing_ss

wellbeing_plot <- interact_plot(wellbeing_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Wellbeing",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)

wellbeing_plot
```


```{r}
# 3. Slope differences ENJOYMENT -YES  pvp players have a more positive realtionship with enjoyment
enjoyment_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(enjoyment ~ Hours * player_type))

enjoyment_ss <- sim_slopes(enjoyment_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)
enjoyment_ss

enjoyment_plot <- interact_plot(enjoyment_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Enjoyment",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)
enjoyment_plot
```

```{r}
# 3. Slope differences EXTRINSIC  --- NO not significant
extrinsic_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(extrinsic ~ Hours * player_type))

extrinsic_ss <- sim_slopes(extrinsic_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

extrinsic_ss

extrinsic_plot <- interact_plot(extrinsic_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Extrinsic motivation",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)

extrinsic_plot
```


```{r}
# 3. Slope differences COMPETENCE  -NO not significant
competence_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(competence ~ Hours * player_type))

competence_ss <- sim_slopes(competence_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)
competence_ss

competence_plot <- interact_plot(competence_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Competence",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)
competence_plot
```

```{r}
# 3. Slope differences AUTONOMY  --- NO not significant
autonomy_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(autonomy ~ Hours * player_type))

autonomy_ss <- sim_slopes(autonomy_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

autonomy_ss

autonomy_plot <- interact_plot(autonomy_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="Play time (Hours)",
                  y.label ="Autonomy",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)

autonomy_plot
```

```{r}
# 3. Slope differences RELATEDNESS - yes (pvp players have a lot stronger relatedness)
relatedness_model <- with(pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ Hours * player_type + gender))

relatedness_ss <- sim_slopes(relatedness_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE)

relatedness_ss

relatedness_plot <- interact_plot(relatedness_model,
                  pred=Hours,
                  modx = player_type,
                  johnson_neyman=FALSE,
                  x.label="",
                  y.label ="Relatedness",
                  legend.main = "Player type",
                  vary.lty = FALSE)

relatedness_plot
```

```{r}
library(gridExtra)
library(ggpubr)

```

```{r}
figure3 <- ggarrange(wellbeing_plot, enjoyment_plot, extrinsic_plot, competence_plot, autonomy_plot, relatedness_plot,
                    ncol = 3, nrow = 2, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "right")
figure3
```

```{r}
# maybe now filter on only pvp players

# subset df for players who played pvp
pvp_pvz_df_nona <- filter(pvz_df_nona, cluster_Hours == 1)
pve_pvz_df_nona <- filter(pvz_df_nona, cluster_Hours == 2)

```



## Univariate figures split by player type 

This figure shows the distribution of well-being and motivation scores for both studies

```{r univariate-scales}
tmp <- dat %>%
  pivot_longer(c(spane_balance, autonomy:extrinsic))
tmp <- tmp %>% 
  mutate(
    name = factor(
      name, 
      levels = c(
        "spane_balance", "extrinsic", "enjoyment", 
        "autonomy", "competence", "relatedness"
      ),
      labels = c(
        "Player well-being", "Extrinsic motivation", "Intrinsic motivation", 
        "Player autonomy", "Player competence", "Player relatedness"
      )
    )
  )
tmp2 <- tmp %>% 
  group_by(Game, name) %>% 
  summarise(value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE))
filter(tmp, Game == "PvZ") %>%  
  ggplot(aes(value, col = Game, fill = Game)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-1, 1)) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = rev(colors), aesthetics = c("color", "fill"),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
    ) +
  geom_histogram(
    aes(y = stat(ncount)),
    col = "white", bins = 20
    ) +
  geom_histogram(
    data = filter(tmp, Game == "AC:NH"),
    aes(y = stat(ncount)*-1), col = "white", bins = 20
    ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = c(-1, -.5, 0, .5, 1), labels = c(1, .5, 0, .5, 1)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous("Value", breaks = pretty_breaks()) +
  geom_point(
    data = tmp2, aes(y = -1.01),
    shape = 25, size = 2.5
    ) +
  # Make sure 1 is shown on all figures by drawing a blank geom
  geom_blank(data = tibble(Game = "PvZ", value = 1)) +
  labs(y = "Normalized count") +
  facet_wrap("name", scales = "free") +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(), legend.position = "right")
```

```{r}


modelpvp <- with(pvp_pvz_df_nona, lm(spane_game_balance ~ pvp_Hours + pvp_kd))
summary(modelpvp)

```
```{r}
